Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 10 2025 at 15:35):

Regarding #theory: applied category theory > Graphs with polarities @ 💬 , For the monoid (N,×,1)(\mathbb{N}, \times, 1)(N,×,1), let us consider the N\mathbb{N}N-graphs. Let G:=v→20vG:= v \xrightarrow{20}vG:=v20​v. Then, a Kleisli morphism Z ⁣:G→T(H)Z \colon G \to T(H)Z:G→T(H), where H:=v→2w→2x→5vH:= v \xrightarrow{2}w \xrightarrow{2}x\xrightarrow{5}vH:=v2​w2​x5​v gives a factorisation of 202020.

I think more generally, for any n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N, if we consider G:=v→nvG:= v \xrightarrow{n}vG:=vn​v, then every prime factorisation n=p1α1p2α2⋯pkαkn=p^{\alpha_1}_1 p^{\alpha_2}_2 \cdots p^{\alpha_k}_{k}n=p1α1​​p2α2​​⋯pkαk​​ of nnn can be described as a (monic)Kleisli morphism Z ⁣:G→T(H)Z \colon G \to T(H)Z:G→T(H) from GGG to some graph HHH, where TTT is the monad associated to free-forgetful adjunction between N\mathbb{N}N-graphs and N\mathbb{N}N-categories?

Of course, it may not be anything interesting.

Kevin Carlson (Apr 10 2025 at 17:45):

John Baez said:

  • What kind of 'nice category' is the 1-category Cat\mathsf{Cat}Cat, such that any slice category Cat/C\mathsf{Cat}/CCat/C is also nice in this way?

Locally finitely presentable (which implies complete and cocomplete and monadic over presheaf category) and extensive is about all I've got.

John Baez (Apr 10 2025 at 18:09):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

Thank you so much for polishing the proof.

Sure!

The proof now looks much, much better than what I did.

It's based on what you did, but I want to explain things to readers who may not know all the concepts well enough. When writing it's always important to imagine who the audience is. I'm imagining an audience of people who want to apply category theory, but may not know very much category theory. So just saying some functor is a discrete fibration may not mean much to them, so I wanted to explain what it means.

Also I think it may someday be useful for people to have an explicit description of the category of LLL-labelled graphs as a presheaf category, so I added that. Probably I should move it out of the proof and make it a separate paragraph.

I am slowly understanding what you meant by "to add the right amount of details" and in "a crispy way".

Great! By the way, I said "crisp", not "crispy". English is a weird language, almost impossible to learn: potato chips (which the British call "crisps") are crispy; fresh lettuce is not crispy - but it's crisp. "Crisp" also means something else:

A crisp way of speaking, writing, or behaving is quick, confident, and effective. For example:

  • a crisp reply

  • a crisp, efficient manner

John Baez (Apr 10 2025 at 18:11):

Kevin Carlson said:

John Baez said:

  • What kind of 'nice category' is the 1-category Cat\mathsf{Cat}Cat, such that any slice category Cat/C\mathsf{Cat}/CCat/C is also nice in this way?

Locally finitely presentable (which implies complete and cocomplete and monadic over presheaf category) and extensive is about all I've got.

Thanks, that's super-helpful! If I'd thought I might have guessed they were locally finitely presentable, but I would never have thought about 'extensive' since I tend to think of that as applying to categories of 'spaces'.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 10 2025 at 18:14):

John Baez said:

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

Thank you so much for polishing the proof.

Sure!

The proof now looks much, much better than what I did.

It's based on what you did, but it explains things to readers who may not know all the concepts well enough. Also I think it may someday be useful to explicitly describe the category of LLL-labelled graphs as a presheaf category, so I added that.

I am slowly understanding what you meant by "to add the right amount of details" and in "a crispy way".

Great! By the way, I said "crisp", not "crispy". English is a weird language: potato chips are crispy, fresh lettuce is not crispy but it's crisp, but "crisp" also means something else:

A crisp way of speaking, writing, or behaving is quick, confident, and effective. For example:

  • a crisp reply

  • a crisp, efficient manner

Thank you. Yes, I meant `crisp', but somehow, I typed crispy.

Kevin Carlson (Apr 10 2025 at 18:40):

John Baez said:

Thanks, that's super-helpful! If I'd thought I might have guessed they were locally finitely presentable, but I would never have thought about 'extensive' since I tend to think of that as applying to categories of 'spaces'.

Well, I think Grothendieck at least thought that categories are a kind of space!

John Baez (Apr 10 2025 at 20:37):

If you think of categories as simplicial sets with an extra property you can think of them as a kind of space, but it takes a lot of nerve.

John Baez (Apr 10 2025 at 20:50):

Yes, I meant 'crisp', but somehow, I typed crispy.

Okay. I started getting interested in the difference between crisp and crispy and got into an argument with my wife about it. I argued that good potato chips are clearly "crispy", while she says they are "crisp".

I don't know if I can continue to live with someone who has such fundamental disagreements with me.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 00:46):

On a more serious note:

LLL-labeled graphs with L={+,−}L = \{+,-\}L={+,−} are often called 'causal loop diagrams' because people use them to identify 'feedback loops'. I think it would be good to say a bit about this. Here's an idea:

We can define the first homology of a graph GGG with coefficients in LLL, H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G), in two equivalent ways. We can either geometrically realize the graph as a space and take its first homology with Z\mathbb{Z}Z coefficients, or - better - define an abelian group of 'cycles' in GGG, called Z1(G)Z_1(G)Z1​(G), and an abelian group of 'boundaries', called B1(G)B_1(G)B1​(G) and form H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) as a quotient:

H1(G)=Z1(G)/B1(G)H_1(G) = Z_1(G)/B_1(G)H1​(G)=Z1​(G)/B1​(G)

At least when we have a finite graph, H1(G)H^1(G)H1(G) is a free abelian group. But beware: there's no canonical choice for the generators of this free group.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 00:52):

However:

1) GGG is a directed graph, and for our applications the only possible feedback loops are directed loops: that is, paths of edges of the form

v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0 v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​

So, we're not really interested in the usual 1st homology, but only a kind of directed first homology. This should be easy to define, but maybe someone in directed algebraic topology has already defined the directed first homology of a directed graph - so we should find out.

2) In some of our applications LLL is not an abelian group but merely a commutative monoid. So, the usual textbook theory of homology doesn't apply. However, the directed first homology of a graph with coefficients in a commutative monoid should still make sense.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 01:37):

When we understand this stuff, I hope there will be

  • a commutative monoid Z⃗1(G)\vec{Z}_1(G)Z1​(G) of 'directed cycles' in GGG

and

  • a congruence relation saying when two directed cycles are 'homologous'

so we can define the commutative monoid H⃗1(G)\vec{H}_1(G)H1​(G) as Z⃗1(G)\vec{Z}_1(G)Z1​(G) modulo the congruence relation of being homologous.

(Note that with commutative monoids, we should mod out by [[congruence relations]], not submonoids! For abelian groups you can turn congruence relations into (normal) subgroups, which lets you talk about modding out by a subgroup. But that uses subtraction!)

I conjecture that, at least for a finite graph, H⃗1(G)\vec{H}_1(G)H1​(G) will be a free commutative monoid on some set of generators. (If you draw me a directed graph, I believe I can find such generators.)

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 01:42):

Then I hope we can do this:

Suppose LLL is a commutative monoid and GGG is an LLL-labeled graph, i.e. a graph with a map ℓ\ellℓ sending edges to elements of LLL.

Clearly for any directed loop in GGG

v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​

we get an element of LLL, namely

ℓ(e1)+⋯+ℓ(en) \ell(e_1) + \cdots + \ell(e_n)ℓ(e1​)+⋯+ℓ(en​)

This describes the feedback around this directed loop. (It's analogous to a holonomy.)

But I also conjecture that we get a map of commutative monoids

ℓ~ ⁣:Z⃗1(G)→L \tilde{\ell} \colon \vec{Z}_1(G) \to L ℓ~:Z1​(G)→L

sending cycles to elements of LLL. I haven't defined cycles, but a cycle should an N\mathbb{N}N-linear combination of edges of GGG with some property saying its boundary is zero. Then, to define $\tilde{\ell}$$ on a cycle, we sum up the labels over all the edges that appear in this cycle. (The same edge may show up several times in a cycle - so then we sum its label several times.)

This tells us the total feedback around any cycle!

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 01:43):

I conjecture that if c∼c′c \sim c'c∼c′ are homologous cycles, then we get the same element of ℓ\ellℓ this way:

ℓ~(c)=ℓ~(c′) \tilde{\ell}(c) = \tilde{\ell}(c') ℓ~(c)=ℓ~(c′)

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 01:47):

If so, ℓ~\tilde{\ell}ℓ~ descends to a map of commutative monoids

H⃗1(G)→L \vec{H}_1(G) \to L H1​(G)→L

Maybe I'll call this by the same name, ℓ~\tilde{\ell}ℓ~.

I conjecture that this map contains all the information about how much feedback there is around any directed loop.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 01:56):

One nice thing about this little project is that @Adittya Chaudhuri has already studied holonomies of connections, and this is very much like that: made simpler because we are working on a graph, but more complicated because we are working with a (commutative) monoid instead of a group!

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 01:57):

It's possible we should develop this for a not-necessarily-commutative monoid.

James Deikun (Apr 11 2025 at 05:24):

In the conventional first homology of a graph, there are no bounding cycles, so H1(G)≅Z1(G)H_1(G) \cong Z_1(G)H1​(G)≅Z1​(G). And when you only look at directed cycles, two cycles can't add up to a cycle that doesn't self-intersect, so you actually get canonical generators.

I was also going to suggest matroid theory besides homology, but it actually doesn't look very promising for this.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 11 2025 at 12:21):

John Baez said:

One nice thing about this little project is that Adittya Chaudhuri has already studied holonomies of connections, and this is very much like that: made simpler because we are working on a graph, but more complicated because we are working with a (commutative) monoid instead of a group!

Thanks. Are you hinting towards the following?

Given a connection 1-form ω\omegaω on a smooth principal GGG-bundle π ⁣:E→M\pi \colon E \to Mπ:E→M and a point x∈Mx \in Mx∈M,

1) we can construct a holonomy map H ⁣:Ω(M,x)→G \mathcal{H} \colon \Omega (M,x) \to GH:Ω(M,x)→G, where Ω(M,x)\Omega (M,x)Ω(M,x) is the set of smooth loops on MMM based at xxx . On the other hand, one can recover the whole bundle with the connection data from the holonomy map.

2) when two smooth loops γ1\gamma_1γ1​ and γ2\gamma_2γ2​ are thin homotopic then the map H\mathcal{H}H descends to a map on the quotient Hˉ ⁣:Ω(M,x)/∼→G \bar{\mathcal{H}} \colon \Omega (M,x)/ \sim \to GHˉ:Ω(M,x)/∼→G. Here, ∼\sim∼ is the equivalence relation on Ω(M,x)\Omega (M,x)Ω(M,x) defined by thin homotopy.

I think (1) is analogous to l~ ⁣:Z⃗1(G)→L \tilde{l} \colon \vec{Z}_1(G) \to L l~:Z1​(G)→L.

I think (2) is analogous to the map l~ ⁣:H⃗1(G)→L\tilde{l} \colon \vec{H}_1(G) \to L l~:H1​(G)→L.

Now, if we replace the set Ω(M,x)\Omega (M,x)Ω(M,x) by Ω(M,x)lazy\Omega (M,x)_{lazy}Ω(M,x)lazy​ containing lazy paths (as defined in https://arxiv.org/pdf/1003.4485), then we can turn Ω(M,x)lazy/∼\Omega (M,x)_{lazy}/ \simΩ(M,x)lazy​/∼ into a group under concatenation as a binary operation. We can then extend the set map Hˉ\bar{\mathcal{H}}Hˉ to a group homomorphism Hˉ ⁣:Ω(M,x)lazy/∼→G\bar{\mathcal{H}} \colon \Omega (M,x)_{lazy}/ \sim \to GHˉ:Ω(M,x)lazy​/∼→G.

Then like the way the group homomorphism Hˉ ⁣:Ω(M,x)lazy/∼→G\bar{\mathcal{H}} \colon \Omega (M,x)_{lazy}/ \sim \to GHˉ:Ω(M,x)lazy​/∼→G contain information of the bundle π ⁣:E→M\pi \colon E \to Mπ:E→M with connection ω\omegaω, the homomorphism of commutative monoids l~ ⁣:H⃗1(G)→L\tilde{l} \colon \vec{H}_1(G) \to L l~:H1​(G)→L contain the information of how much feedback in any directed loop in the LLL-labeled graph GGG.

Or, am I misunderstanding ?

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 11 2025 at 14:34):

@John Baez The relation (that you described) between the directed first homology of a graph with coefficients in a (commutative) monoid LLL and the feedback loops in LLL-labeled graphs seems super interesting!! Now, I am trying to understand all these things (what you wrote) properly.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 11 2025 at 14:48):

By directed Algebraic topology, are you saying about https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Directed+Algebraic+Topology?

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 16:51):

James Deikun said:

In the conventional first homology of a graph, there are no bounding cycles, so H1(G)≅Z1(G)H_1(G) \cong Z_1(G)H1​(G)≅Z1​(G).

In Sunada's approach to undirected graphs (summarized in my paper Topological crystals, section 2) one counts an edge path like

v0→e1v1→e2v2→e2−1v1→e1−1v0 v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} v_2 \xrightarrow{e_2^{-1}} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_1^{-1}} v_0 v0​e1​​v1​e2​​v2​e2−1​​v1​e1−1​​v0​

as a 1-cycle that's homologous to zero. Maybe a more "efficient" approach wouldn't even allow such 1-cycles, but there's something nice about making every edge path that's a loop give a cycle.

But more importantly, you're right that in a directed graph, we're only interested in directed paths, so edges don't have inverses, and this issue goes away!

And when you only look at directed cycles, two cycles can't add up to a cycle that doesn't self-intersect, so you actually get canonical generators.

Another great point! I had not yet mentally adjusted from the case of undirected graphs.

This is very nice for the theory of causal loop diagrams, and it must already be known in the system dynamics community. A canonical set of 'generating' feedback loops is a very pleasant thing.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:05):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

Then like the way the group homomorphism Hˉ ⁣:Ω(M,x)lazy/∼→G\bar{\mathcal{H}} \colon \Omega (M,x)_{lazy}/ \sim \to GHˉ:Ω(M,x)lazy​/∼→G contain information of the bundle π ⁣:E→M\pi \colon E \to Mπ:E→M with connection ω\omegaω, the homomorphism of commutative monoids l~ ⁣:H⃗1(G)→L\tilde{l} \colon \vec{H}_1(G) \to L l~:H1​(G)→L contain the information of how much feedback in any directed loop in the LLL-labeled graph GGG.

Or, am I misunderstanding ?

No, that's exactly the correct analogy. This analogy has been on my mind for many decades, because starting in the early 1990s, before working on higher gauge theory, I worked on loop quantum gravity. Thus, long before treating connections on a trivial GGG-bundle over a manifold MMM as smooth functors from the path groupoid of MMM to GGG, I wrote some papers about connections on graphs, which took a similar viewpoint. See for example

  • Spin network states in gauge theory, sec. 2: Gauge theory on a graph.

It's a bit primitive since it doesn't introduce the holonomy of a path, but you can figure out what that must be!

I'm reusing those ideas now, but a bunch of things change when we have a directed graph, where paths are only allowed to move in the direction of the edges.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 11 2025 at 17:08):

Thanks!! Sounds super interesting!

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:08):

By the way, you read all my messages too quickly, so please reread that.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:09):

I usually rewrite my posts a few times. Sorry!

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:13):

Anyway, here is how it works for a directed graph GGG - the kind of graph we're talking about in our paper. Suppose LLL is a monoid. A connection on a trivial LLL-bundle over GGG should clearly be a functor

Π⃗1(G)→BL \vec{\Pi}_1(G) \to \mathsf{B} LΠ1​(G)→BL

where Π⃗1(G)\vec{\Pi}_1(G)Π1​(G) is the 'fundamental category' of GGG and BL\mathsf{B} LBL is the one-object category corresponding to LLL. We get a fundamental category instead of a fundamental groupoid because we're not allowed walk backward along a directed edge. The fundamental category has

  • vertices as objects
  • directed edge paths as morphisms

But look - this 'fundamental category' is exactly the same as the free category on the graph GGG, which we call Free(G)\mathsf{Free}(G)Free(G) in our paper and study in detail!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 11 2025 at 17:14):

Now, I got your point. And, sorry for reading the last message quickly.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 11 2025 at 17:18):

In a way, LLL-labeled category P ⁣:Free(G)→BLP \colon Free(G) \to BLP:Free(G)→BL is a connection on a trivial LLL-bundle over GGG ?

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:19):

It's okay to read my message quickly... but I never expect anyone to read my messages quickly, so I tend to keep rewriting them and improving them. I find it easier to write messages to you later in the day, when you're asleep and I can rewrite them dozens of times.

So, where was I?

A connection on the trivial LLL-bundle over GGG is a functor

Free(G)→BL \mathsf{Free}(G) \to \mathsf{B} L Free(G)→BL

But this is exactly a way of making Free(G)\mathsf{Free}(G)Free(G) into an LLL-labeled category! This also corresponds precisely to a way of making GGG into an LLL-labeled graph.

In a way, LLL-labeled category P ⁣:Free(G)→BLP \colon \mathsf{Free}(G) \to BLP:Free(G)→BL is a connection on a trivial LLL-bundle over GGG?

Exactly.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 11 2025 at 17:20):

John Baez said:

It's okay to read my message quickly... but I never expect anyone to read my messages quickly, so I tend to keep rewriting them and improving them. I find it easier to write messages to you later in the day, when you're asleep and I can rewrite them dozens of times.

I got your point.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:26):

So, we don't need to talk a lot about connections on graphs; they are just our LLL-labeled graphs.

There's more to say when LLL is a commutative monoid. When LLL is noncommutative, the holonomy around a loop depends on the basepoint. (When LLL is a group, the holonomy gets conjugated when we change the basepoint.) But when LLL is a commutative monoid, the holonomy doesn't depend on the basepoint!

More generally, when LLL is commutative we can talk about the holonomy around a collection of loops in GGG: it's just the sum of the holonomies around each loop... and the sum is well-defined because LLL is commutative.

Indeed, we can talk about the holonomy around any cycle in GGG. Thus, when LLL is a commutative monoid, for any LLL-labeled graph GGG we expect to get a homomorphism of commutative monoids

H1(G)→L H_1(G) \to LH1​(G)→L

James Deikun (Apr 11 2025 at 17:28):

Note that also when a noncommutative monoid has a cyclic trace, you can use the cyclic trace to form a kind of basepoint-independent holonomy on loops specifically.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:31):

Good point. The funny thing is that in applications to "system dynamics", like epidemiology and systems biology, the monoid always seems to be commutative. So we'll probably focus on that case.

You pointed out something which @Adittya Chaudhuri and I should state and prove: since our graph GGG is directed graph, the commutative monoid I'm calling H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is free on a canonical set of generators, the non-self-intersecting cycles.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 17:33):

We should make up some nice term for them, like the 'basic feedback loops'. There is probably already a standard term for them in the field of system dynamics.

So, any LLL-labeled graph gives a commutative monoid homomorphism

H1(G)→L H_1(G) \to LH1​(G)→L

but this is determined by its value on the 'basic feedback loops', i.e. the canonical generators of the free commutative monoid H1(G)→LH_1(G) \to LH1​(G)→L.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 21:57):

By the way, I'm pretty sure any free commutative monoid has a canonical set of generators. Consider the finitely generated case: a free commutative monoid is isomorphic to (Nn,+)(\mathbb{N}^n, +)(Nn,+). This is free on the nnn generators

e1=(1,0,0,… ),e2=(0,1,0,… ),…e_1 = (1, 0, 0, \dots ), \quad e_2 = (0, 1, 0, \dots ), \dots e1​=(1,0,0,…),e2​=(0,1,0,…),…

and it's not free on any other generators: each eie_iei​ cannot be expressed as a sum of elements except itself and the zero vector.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 22:04):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

By directed algebraic topology, are you saying about https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Directed+Algebraic+Topology?

Yes. You'll see that there are lots of approaches to directed algebraic topology. As far as I know, none of them has triumphed yet.

The idea (at least according to me) is that just as a homotopy nnn-type is essentially the same as an nnn-groupoid, a directed nnn-type should be essentially the same as an nnn-category. But it should be some sort of space with extra structure that has a 'fundamental nnn-category'. Thus, directed nnn-types should give a more topological way of thinking about nnn-categories, just as homotopy types give a more topological way of thinking about nnn-groupoids.

But we don't need to think in detail about this complicated stuff: it's just a guiding philosophy. Directed graphs should be a nice way to think about directed 1-types, and we already know how to get the 'fundamental category' of a directed graph GGG: it's what our paper calls Free(G)\textsf{Free}(G)Free(G).

We probably shouldn't talk much about directed algebraic topology in our paper; it's just good to keep in mind. The concept of the 'directed first homology monoid' of a (directed) graph seems somewhat useful, though.

John Baez (Apr 11 2025 at 23:49):

Minor stuff:

  • I think I will change "morphism of graphs", "morphism of LLL-labeled graphs", "morphism of LLL-labeled categories" to "map of graphs", "map of LLL-labeled graphs", "map of LLL-labeled categories". The word "morphism" is showing up a bit too often, e.g. an LLL-labeled category already has morphisms.
  • I'm starting to write a section on motifs and Kleisli maps between LLL-labeled graphs.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 03:25):

John Baez said:

The idea (at least according to me) is that just as a homotopy nnn-type is essentially the same as an nnn-groupoid, a directed nnn-type should be essentially the same as an nnn-category. But it should be some sort of space with extra structure that has a 'fundamental nnn-category'. Thus, directed nnn-types should give a more topological way of thinking about nnn-categories, just as homotopy types give a more topological way of thinking about nnn-groupoids.

Interesting. In a way, are you are talking about a directed version of the homotopy hypothesis https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/homotopy+hypothesis, and thus a fundamental (∞,1)(\infty,1)(∞,1)-category https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/fundamental+%28infinity%2C1%29-category should capture the information of a directed topological space?

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 04:47):

In section 2.3 (overleaf file), you mentioned a relation between `motifs in music' and motifs in LLL-labeled graphs. I was not much aware of this relation till today, and I just checked https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Motif_(music) to learn more about it. It's very interesting, and after reading the Wikipedia article, I also feel it reflects the same meaning. "Some small signature tunes within a big musical piece somehow repeat many times in the big piece". Surprisingly, I think listeners (especially non-professional musicians) also sometimes try to identify the whole big piece by only remembering/humming the small signature tunes or motifs in the big piece.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 04:58):

Right! I believe the word 'motif' comes from music, made especially famous by Wagner's use of leitmofits. Grothendieck used this word in mathematics - 'motive' is French for 'motif'.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 05:02):

Thanks. Interesting.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 05:03):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

John Baez said:

The idea (at least according to me) is that just as a homotopy nnn-type is essentially the same as an nnn-groupoid, a directed nnn-type should be essentially the same as an nnn-category. But it should be some sort of space with extra structure that has a 'fundamental nnn-category'. Thus, directed nnn-types should give a more topological way of thinking about nnn-categories, just as homotopy types give a more topological way of thinking about nnn-groupoids.

Interesting. In a way, are you are talking about a directed version of the homotopy hypothesis https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/homotopy+hypothesis, and thus a fundamental (∞,1)(\infty,1)(∞,1)-category https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/fundamental+%28infinity%2C1%29-category should capture the information of a directed topological space?

I was indeed talking about a directed version of the homotopy hypothesis. But I was talking about it for nnn-categories, or probably better for (∞,n)(\infty,n)(∞,n)-categories. There should be different kinds of directed space. In some the paths can be directed, i.e. non-invertible, but the homotopies between paths, etc. are all invertible. This kind of directed space should have a fundamental (∞,1)(\infty,1)(∞,1)-category.

But people also study directed spaces where the homotopies are also directed, so you can have a homotopy from one path γ\gammaγ to another path δ\deltaδ but not from δ\deltaδ back to γ\gammaγ. If all homotopies between homotopies, etc., are invertible then these spaces would have a fundamental (∞,2)(\infty,2)(∞,2)-category. And so on.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 05:05):

Thanks. I understand your point.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 05:05):

By the way, if you want to link to the nLab here you don't have to include a URL like https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/homotopy+hypothesis. There's a more elegant way: you can just type the page title in double square brackets, like this: [[homotopy hypothesis]]. Some smart person added this feature.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 05:07):

John Baez said:

By the way, if you want to link to the nLab here you don't have to include a URL like https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/homotopy+hypothesis. There's a more elegant way: you can just type the page title in double square brackets, like this: [[homotopy hypothesis]].

Thank you. Yes. I wanted to do it. But I was not aware "how to do it" until now.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 05:08):

You can also include arbitrary URLs like this:

[Motivating motives](http://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/motives/)

gives

Motivating motives

But for the nLab you just need to put the page title in double square brackets.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 05:10):

Thank you. I will do this.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 15:06):

Today, I was trying to understand the things from the general perspective discussed before. I am just trying to write down what I am thinking.

1)There is a well-known adjunction between the category of topological spaces Top\mathsf{Top}Top and the category of simplicial sets sSet\mathsf{sSet}sSet given by singular simplicial set Sing ⁣:Top→sSetSing \colon \mathsf{Top} \to \mathsf{sSet}Sing:Top→sSet and geometric realization functor ∣−∣ ⁣:sSet→Top|-| \colon \mathsf{sSet} \to \mathsf{Top}∣−∣:sSet→Top which connects the study of topological spaces via simplicial sets. Now, according to The Homology of Simplicial Sets, given a topological space XXX, replacing Sing(X)nSing(X)_{n}Sing(X)n​ by Z[Sing(X)n]\mathbb{Z}[Sing(X)_{n}]Z[Sing(X)n​] for each nnn, we obtain a simplicial abelian group Z[Sing(X)n]\mathbb{Z}[Sing(X)_{n}]Z[Sing(X)n​]. Now, one can convert the Z[Sing(X)]\mathbb{Z}[Sing(X)]Z[Sing(X)] into a chain complex C∗={Z[Sing(X)n]}n≥0C_{*}=\lbrace \mathbb{Z}[Sing(X)_{n}] \rbrace_{n \geq 0}C∗​={Z[Sing(X)n​]}n≥0​ whose boundary maps are given by the alternating sum of the face operators of Z[Sing(X)]\mathbb{Z}[Sing(X)]Z[Sing(X)]. Now, if we compute the homology of the chain complex C∗C{*}C∗ ([[chain homology and cohomology]]), we get the singular homology of XXX in each degree. In particular, in the degree 1, we get the first singular homology group of XXX.

2) On the other hand, there is also a well-known adjunction between Cat\mathsf{Cat}Cat and sSet\mathsf{sSet}sSet given by the nerve functor N ⁣:Cat→sSetN \colon \mathsf{Cat} \to \mathsf{sSet}N:Cat→sSet and its left adjoint homotopy category functor H ⁣:sSet→CatH \colon \mathsf{sSet} \to \mathsf{Cat}H:sSet→Cat which connects the study of categories via simplicial sets. Now, given a topological space XXX, the fundamental groupoid functor π1 ⁣:Top→Gpd\pi_{1} \colon \mathsf{Top} \to \mathsf{Gpd}π1​:Top→Gpd (as explained by Harry Gindi in one of my 5-year-old MO question What is the geometric realization of the the nerve of a fundamental groupoid of a space? ) is isomorphic to H∘SingH \circ SingH∘Sing, and hence, H∘Sing(X)H \circ Sing(X) H∘Sing(X) is same as the fundamental groupoid of XXX i.e π1(X)\pi_{1}(X)π1​(X).

Now, as @John Baez was saying about directed topological spaces, and since he was discussing about homology monoids (which may make sense, because paths in directed topological spaces may not be able to be traversed in the reverse direction ) and fundamental categories (which may make sense, because paths in directed topological spaces may not be able to be traversed in the reverse direction ) of (such spaces), in the context of directed graphs, I was wondering whether one can suitably "directify" the correspondence of (1) and (2) to obtain suitable notions of singular homology monoids and fundamental categories of directed topological spaces. Then, we may obtain the results for directed graphs as a special case.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 15:20):

@John Baez Another thing about which I was also thinking today:

Directed graphs can also be seen as simplicial sets Directed Graphs as Simplicial Sets. Although the definition of a directed graph in Directed Graphs as Simplicial Sets coincides with our definition, the definition of morphisms are little different. However, the fundamental category of these directed graphs are free categories of the graphs as explained in The Path Category of a Directed Graph.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 15:29):

The questions you raise are very interesting.

I was wondering whether one can suitably "directify" the correspondence of (1) and (2) to obtain suitable notions of singular homology monoids and fundamental categories of directed topological spaces. Then, we may obtain the results for directed graphs as a special case.

That's a fascinating and ambitious project and I hope someone tries it - or has already done it. Of course one needs to properly define 'directed topological space', and right now there seem to be multiple competing definitions.

I don't have the energy to tackle this project: I just want to finish our paper, which is much easier. But there may be people here who can help you with this project.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 15:34):

You could say our project is exploring the homotopy theory and homology theory of one-dimensional directed spaces, and applying it to biology and epidemiology. That's ambitious enough for me. :upside_down:

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 16:03):

I understand your point.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 16:07):

John Baez said:

You could say our project is exploring the homotopy theory and homology theory of one-dimensional directed spaces, and applying it to biology and epidemiology.

This is also fascinating. It may give ideas on how to approach the general case. Although I do not know.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 16:08):

John Baez said:

The questions you raise are very interesting.

Thank you!!

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 16:09):

1-dimensional topology is a lot simpler than higher-dimensional topology; that's why we teach kids about the fundamental group long before we teach them about πn\pi_nπn​, and why people understood gauge theory long before higher gauge theory. But I like doing simple things that are actually useful. Often the simplest ideas are the most useful.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 16:15):

I understand your point completely. I think from the point of applications in biology and systems dynamics, a 1-dimensional case would be more suitable now. Then, if we feel that "we need to upgrade our framework for explaining certain realistic phenomenon in biology or systems dynamics" like the way "gauge theory upgraded to higher gauge theory" , then one may develop the general case with a correct motivation. I am also not sure if what I am saying actually makes sense or not.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 16:18):

John Baez said:

Often the simplest ideas are the most useful.

I really like this line and I fully agree to it.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 16:50):

John Baez said:

That's a fascinating and ambitious project and I hope someone tries it - or has already done it. Of course one needs to properly define 'directed topological space', and right now there seem to be multiple competing definitions.

Thank you. Yes, I understand your point regarding "multiple competing definitions" from the nLab page [[directed topological space]].

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 12 2025 at 18:26):

John Baez said:

  • What kind of 'nice category' is the 1-category Cat\mathsf{Cat}Cat, such that any slice category Cat/C\mathsf{Cat}/CCat/C is also nice in this way?

It may not be completely related to our paper, but I just observed this:

1-category Cat\mathsf{Cat}Cat can be given a model structure ([[Thomason model structure]]) and hence, for any category C\mathsf{C}C the slice category Cat/C\mathsf{Cat}/\mathsf{C}Cat/C can be given a [[slice model structure]] in a nice way. One can also do similar with [[canonical model structure on Cat]].

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 21:11):

Nice!

Here's how I'd like to define the first homology monoid of a graph in our paper, taking directedness into account. If there are flaws or mistakes, someone please let me know.

We start with a graph GGG: a set VVV of vertices, a set EEE of edges, and two maps s,t:E→Vs,t: E \to Vs,t:E→V.

  1. Define the commutative monoid of 1-chains C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) to be the free commutative monoid on EEE, which is N[E]\mathbb{N}[E]N[E], the commutative monoid of formal N\mathbb{N}N-linear combinations of elements of EEE.
  2. Define the commutative monoid of 0-chains C0(G))C_0(G))C0​(G)) to be the free commutative monoid on VVV, which is N[V]\mathbb{N}[V]N[V], the commutative monoid of formal N\mathbb{N}N-linear combinations of elements of VVV.
  3. Note we have set inclusions E→C1(G)E \to C_1(G)E→C1​(G), V→C0(G)V \to C_0(G)V→C0​(G). Define maps s,t:C1(G)→C0(G)s, t: C^1(G) \to C^0(G)s,t:C1(G)→C0(G) to be the monoid homomorphisms extending s,t:E→Vs,t : E \to Vs,t:E→V.
  4. Define a 1-chain ccc to be a 1-cycle if s(c)=t(c)s(c) = t(c)s(c)=t(c).
  5. Define H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) to be the commutative monoid of 1-cycles. (We should use cycles modulo boundaries, but the only boundary is 000.)

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 23:08):

Note that we can generalize this to define the first homology of a graph GGG with coefficients in any commutative monoid LLL:

  1. Define the commutative monoid of 1-chains C1(G,L)C_1(G,L)C1​(G,L) to be L[E]L[E]L[E], the commutative monoid of formal LLL-linear combinations of elements of EEE.
  2. Define the commutative monoid of 0-chains C0(G,L)C_0(G,L)C0​(G,L) to be L[V]L[V]L[V], the commutative monoid of formal LLL-linear combinations of elements of VVV.
  3. Note we have inclusions of sets E→C1(G,L)E \to C_1(G,L)E→C1​(G,L), V→C2(G,L)V \to C_2(G,L)V→C2​(G,L). Define maps s,t:C1(G,L)→C0(G,L)s, t: C^1(G,L) \to C^0(G,L)s,t:C1(G,L)→C0(G,L) to be the monoid homomorphisms extending s,t:E→Vs,t : E \to Vs,t:E→V.
  4. Define a 1-chain ccc to be a 1-cycle if s(c)=t(c)s(c) = t(c)s(c)=t(c).
  5. Define H1(G,L)H_1(G,L)H1​(G,L) to be the commutative monoid of 1-cycles. (We should use cycles modulo boundaries, but again the only boundary is 000.)

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 23:14):

But I don't think we need H1(G,L)H_1(G,L)H1​(G,L) now: it seems for applications what matters is H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) (which is the same as H1(G,N)H_1(G,\mathbb{N})H1​(G,N).

The reason is that we want to prove this:

Theorem. H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is a free commutative monoid. There is a unique set of generators ci∈H1(G)c_i \in H_1(G)ci​∈H1​(G) such that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is free on the set {ci}\{c_i\}{ci​}.

If we're doing applied mathematics, we can call these generators the basic feedback loops.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 23:24):

Lemma. Let LLL be a commutative monoid. Then every map ℓ ⁣:E→G\ell \colon E \to Gℓ:E→G (called an LLL-labeling) extends uniquely to a monoid homomorphism C1(G)→L C_1(G) \to LC1​(G)→L, which we also call ℓ\ellℓ.

Notice that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is a sub-monoid of C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G), unlike what we usually expect in homology. ℓ\ellℓ thus restricts to a map H1(G)→LH_1(G) \to LH1​(G)→L, which we again call ℓ\ellℓ. By the theorem this map ℓ ⁣:H1(G)→L\ell \colon H_1(G) \to Lℓ:H1​(G)→L is uniquely determined by its values on the basic feedback loops!

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 23:25):

The moral: a labeling of a graph lets us assign a kind of 'feedback' valued in LLL to any cycle in the graph, but these feedbacks are determined by the feedbacks on the basic feedback loops.

John Baez (Apr 12 2025 at 23:35):

(By the way, while I defined 1st homology with coefficients in the commutative monoid LLL, it looks like what we're secretly using here is 1st cohomology with coefficients in LLL. This makes me realize that when L=RL = \mathbb{R}L=R, we can think of an ℓ\ellℓ-labeling of a graph as assigning a voltage to each edge. This sets up a connection between electrical circuits and cohomology of graphs, which I've explained here). That choice of LLL is an abelian group, and right now I'm mostly excited about the generalization to commutative monoids. But still, our paper should mention electrical circuits as an example.)

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 05:14):

Thanks!! I am trying to understand your construction. Just a very minor point: In the begining at point 2, I think there is a typo. I think you meant C0(G)=N[V]C_{0}(G)=\mathbb{N}[V]C0​(G)=N[V] ? Also, in point 3, I think by C2(G)C_2(G)C2​(G) you meant C0(G)C_0(G)C0​(G)?

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 05:25):

Yes, and yes. You're right! I'll fix those mistakes.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 12:32):

I may be completely misunderstanding, but I am trying to write down what I am thinking:

According to your construction, given a graph G=[s,t ⁣:E→V]G=[s,t \colon E \to V]G=[s,t:E→V], consider a cycle c=α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkekc= \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k}c=α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​ in C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G). Hence, by the way you defined a cycle in the point (3), we must have s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c), which implies s(ei)=t(ei)s(e_i)=t(e_i)s(ei​)=t(ei​) for all i∈1,2,…,ki \in 1,2, \ldots, ki∈1,2,…,k. Then, this should imply each of the edge eie_iei​ is a loop. Now, if I assume the correctness of the theorem you stated, then we can assume the edges eie_iei​ are basic feedback loops for each eie_iei​. This also says, every basic feedback loop is an edge (not a path).

Intuitively, (according to me), it means any cycle is composed of basic feedback loops, and the coeffcients attached to basic feedback loop eie_iei​ say the number of times eie_iei​ is counted in ccc. However, if I consider a cycle cˉ\bar{c}cˉ like this: cˉ:=a→e1b→e2c→e3a\bar{c}:= a \xrightarrow{e_1}b \xrightarrow{e_2}c \xrightarrow{e_3}acˉ:=ae1​​be2​​ce3​​a, where a,b,ca,b,ca,b,c are distinct, then I am not able to guess how can I write cˉ\bar{c}cˉ in terms of basic feed back loops. (Also, I am not completely understanding the intuition behind the coefficients of the basic feedback loops).

May be I am misunderstanding something very fundamentally.

Now, if we slightly modify to the following:

Consider a graph G=[s,t ⁣:E→V]G=[s,t \colon E \to V]G=[s,t:E→V] as before, and now consider the category Free(G)\mathsf{Free}(G)Free(G). Let us denote the object set and morphism set by respectively, VVV and Path(G)Path(G)Path(G). Now, I am repeating the same construction as you did. More precisely,

  1. Define the commutative monoid of 1-chains C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) to be the free commutative monoid on Path(G)Path(G)Path(G), which is N[Path(G)]\mathbb{N}[Path(G)]N[Path(G)], the commutative monoid of formal N\mathbb{N}N-linear combinations of elements of Path(G)Path(G)Path(G).
  2. Define the commutative monoid of 0-chains C0(G))C_0(G))C0​(G)) to be the free commutative monoid on VVV, which is N[V]\mathbb{N}[V]N[V], the commutative monoid of formal N\mathbb{N}N-linear combinations of elements of VVV.
  3. Note we have set inclusions Path(G)→C1(G)Path(G) \to C_1(G)Path(G)→C1​(G), V→C0(G)V \to C_0(G)V→C0​(G). Define maps s,t:C1(G)→C0(G)s, t: C_1(G) \to C_0(G)s,t:C1​(G)→C0​(G) to be the monoid homomorphisms extending s,t:Path(G)→Vs,t : Path(G) \to Vs,t:Path(G)→V.
  4. Define a 1-chain ccc to be a 1-cycle if s(c)=t(c)s(c) = t(c)s(c)=t(c).
  5. Define H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) to be the commutative monoid of 1-cycles.

Now, in the light of the above modified definition, consider a cycle c=α1p1+α2p2+⋯+αkpkc= \alpha_1p_1 + \alpha_2p_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}p_{k}c=α1​p1​+α2​p2​+⋯+αk​pk​ in C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) (each pip_ipi​ is a path or a sequence of edges). The condition of cycle then forces each pip_ipi​ to be a graph theoretic cycle (usual cycle in graph theory). Now, if I consider the cycle cˉ:=a→e1b→e2c→e3a\bar{c}:= a \xrightarrow{e_1}b \xrightarrow{e_2}c \xrightarrow{e_3}acˉ:=ae1​​be2​​ce3​​a, then the whole cˉ\bar{c}cˉ itself is a basic feedback loop (not decomposable) and hence, a generator of H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G).

Intuition of the modified definition:

Intuitively, (according to me), it means any cycle is composed of basic feedback loops, and the coeffcients attached to basic feedback loop pip_ipi​ say the number of times pip_ipi​ is counted in ccc. I think in the light of the category Free(G)Free(G)Free(G), the word counted is also making sense.

Now, I am trying to see, how the Lemma part gets modified:

Consider a map ℓ ⁣:E→L\ell \colon E \to Lℓ:E→L (called an LLL-labeling) . Now, by free-forgetful adjunction between graphs and categories, the set Hom(G,GL)≅Hom(Free(G),BL){\rm{Hom}}(G,G_L{}) \cong {\rm{Hom}}(Free(G), BL)Hom(G,GL​)≅Hom(Free(G),BL). Now, since C1(G)=N[Path(G)]C_1(G)=\mathbb{N}[Path(G)]C1​(G)=N[Path(G)], we should have a Lemma(modified) as follows:

Lemma(modified). Let LLL be a commutative monoid. Then every map ℓ ⁣:E→L\ell \colon E \to Lℓ:E→L (called an LLL-labeling) extends uniquely to a monoid homomorphism C1(G)→L C_1(G) \to LC1​(G)→L, which we also call ℓ\ellℓ.

But, it may not be a monoid homomorphism!!

Reason: Say, γ=(α1p1+α2p2+⋯+αkpk)↦∑i=1kl(pi)\gamma=( \alpha_1p_1 + \alpha_2p_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}p_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(p_i)γ=(α1​p1​+α2​p2​+⋯+αk​pk​)↦∑i=1k​l(pi​), where l(pi=e1e2…en):=l(e1)+l(e2)+…+l(en)l(p_i=e_1e_2 \ldots e_n):=l(e_1)+l(e_2) +\ldots + l(e_n)l(pi​=e1​e2​…en​):=l(e1​)+l(e2​)+…+l(en​) . Similarly, γ′=(α1′p1+α2′p2+⋯+αk′pk)↦∑i=1kl(pi)\gamma'=( \alpha'_1p_1 + \alpha'_2p_2 + \cdots + \alpha'_{k}p_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(p_i)γ′=(α1′​p1​+α2′​p2​+⋯+αk′​pk​)↦∑i=1k​l(pi​). But (γ+γ′)↦∑i=1kl(pi)(\gamma + \gamma') \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(p_i)(γ+γ′)↦∑i=1k​l(pi​), which may not be equal to ∑i=1kl(pi)+∑i=1kl(pi)\sum^{k}_{i=1}l(p_i)+ \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(p_i)∑i=1k​l(pi​)+∑i=1k​l(pi​).

But the same reason also holds true on the non-modified version.

However, I think if we replace C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) by C1(G,L)C_1(G,L)C1​(G,L) and assume the commutative monoid (L,+,0)(L,+,0)(L,+,0) is a part of a rig (L,+,0,×,1)(L, +, 0, \times, 1)(L,+,0,×,1) , then from the distribuitive law of a rig, ℓ ⁣:C1(G)→L\ell \colon C_1(G) \to L ℓ:C1​(G)→L can be proved as a homomorphism of monoids/ or may be rigs.

I apologise priorly if I am making very fundamental mistakes.

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 16:42):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

I may be completely misunderstanding, but I am trying to write down what I am thinking:

According to your construction, given a graph G=[s,t ⁣:E→V]G=[s,t \colon E \to V]G=[s,t:E→V], consider a cycle c=α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkekc= \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k}c=α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​ in C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G). Hence, by the way you defined a cycle in the point (3), we must have s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c), which implies s(ei)=t(ei)s(e_i)=t(e_i)s(ei​)=t(ei​) for all i∈1,2,…,ki \in 1,2, \ldots, ki∈1,2,…,k. Then, this should imply each of the edge eie_iei​ is a loop.

If that's true my definition is bad. But let's see. Consider this triangle-shaped graph:

v1→e1v2→e2v3→e3v1 v_1 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_2 \xrightarrow{e_2} v_3 \xrightarrow{e_3} v_1 v1​e1​​v2​e2​​v3​e3​​v1​

I expect that

e1+e2+e3 e_1 + e_2 + e_3 e1​+e2​+e3​

is a cycle, since it goes around the triangle, even though we don't have s(ei)=t(ei)s(e_i) = t(e_i)s(ei​)=t(ei​) as you claim. Let's see:

s(e1+e2+e3)=s(e1)+s(e2)+s(e3)=v1+v2+v3 s(e_1 + e_2 + e_3) = s(e_1) + s(e_2) + s(e_3) = v_1 + v_2 + v_3s(e1​+e2​+e3​)=s(e1​)+s(e2​)+s(e3​)=v1​+v2​+v3​

t(e1+e2+e3)=t(e1)+t(e2)+t(e3)=v2+v3+v1 t(e_1 + e_2 + e_3) = t(e_1) + t(e_2) + t(e_3) = v_2 + v_3 + v_1t(e1​+e2​+e3​)=t(e1​)+t(e2​)+t(e3​)=v2​+v3​+v1​

so yes, e1+e2+e3e_1 + e_2 + e_3e1​+e2​+e3​ is a cycle.

I believe your mistake occurs here:

s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c), which implies s(ei)=t(ei)s(e_i)=t(e_i)s(ei​)=t(ei​) for all i∈1,2,…,ki \in 1,2, \ldots, ki∈1,2,…,k

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 16:46):

There's no way to deduce s(ei)=t(ei)s(e_i) = t(e_i)s(ei​)=t(ei​) from

s(α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkek)= s( \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k}) =s(α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​)=
t(α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkek) t( \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k})t(α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​)

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 16:53):

Yh. Sorry. It was a bad mistake. Somehow, I mixed up!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:05):

John Baez said:

Lemma. Let LLL be a commutative monoid. Then every map ℓ ⁣:E→G\ell \colon E \to Gℓ:E→G (called an LLL-labeling) extends uniquely to a monoid homomorphism C1(G)→L C_1(G) \to LC1​(G)→L, which we also call ℓ\ellℓ.

Without assuming a compatibility, between N\mathbb{N}N and LLL, is it becoming a monoid homomorphism? For example,

Say, γ=(α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkek)↦∑i=1kl(ei)\gamma=( \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)γ=(α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​), . Similarly, γ′=(α1′e1+α2′e2+⋯+αk′ek)↦∑i=1kl(ei)\gamma'=( \alpha'_1e_1 + \alpha'_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha'_{k}e_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)γ′=(α1′​e1​+α2′​e2​+⋯+αk′​ek​)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​). But (γ+γ′)↦∑i=1kl(ei)(\gamma + \gamma') \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)(γ+γ′)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​), which may not be equal to ∑i=1kl(li)+∑i=1kl(li)\sum^{k}_{i=1}l(l_i)+ \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(l_i)∑i=1k​l(li​)+∑i=1k​l(li​).

However, I think if we replace C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) by C1(G,L)C_1(G,L)C1​(G,L) and assume the commutative monoid (L,+,0)(L,+,0)(L,+,0) is a part of a rig (L,+,0,×,1)(L, +, 0, \times, 1)(L,+,0,×,1) , then from the distribuitive law of a rig, ℓ ⁣:C1(G)→L\ell \colon C_1(G) \to L ℓ:C1​(G)→L can be proved as a homomorphism of monoids/ or may be rigs.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:05):
