Yes, I can completely understand.

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 17:06):

Say, γ=(α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkek)↦∑i=1kl(ei)\gamma=( \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)γ=(α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​), . Similarly, γ′=(α1′e1+α2′e2+⋯+αk′ek)↦∑i=1kl(ei)\gamma'=( \alpha'_1e_1 + \alpha'_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha'_{k}e_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)γ′=(α1′​e1​+α2′​e2​+⋯+αk′​ek​)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​). But (γ+γ′)↦∑i=1kl(ei)(\gamma + \gamma') \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)(γ+γ′)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​).

How are you deriving that last two equations?

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:08):

John Baez said:

Say, γ=(α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkek)↦∑i=1kl(ei)\gamma=( \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)γ=(α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​), . Similarly, γ′=(α1′e1+α2′e2+⋯+αk′ek)↦∑i=1kl(ei)\gamma'=( \alpha'_1e_1 + \alpha'_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha'_{k}e_{k}) \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)γ′=(α1′​e1​+α2′​e2​+⋯+αk′​ek​)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​). But (γ+γ′)↦∑i=1kl(ei)(\gamma + \gamma') \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)(γ+γ′)↦∑i=1k​l(ei​).

How are you deriving that last equation?

By the same map : (α1+α1′)e1+(α2+α2′)e2+⋯+(αk+αk′)ek↦∑i=1kl(ei)( \alpha_1 +\alpha'_1)e_1 + (\alpha_2 + \alpha_2')e_2 + \cdots + (\alpha_{k}+ \alpha'_k)e_{k} \mapsto \sum^{k}_{i=1}l(e_i)(α1​+α1′​)e1​+(α2​+α2′​)e2​+⋯+(αk​+αk′​)ek​↦∑i=1k​l(ei​)

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:09):

αi∈N\alpha_i \in \mathbb{N}αi​∈N

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:10):

if αi∈L\alpha_i \in Lαi​∈L as a rig, we can do this by distributive law

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 17:13):

Remember, we're extending the labelling ℓ:E→L\ell : E \to Lℓ:E→L to a monoid homomorphism ℓ:C1(G)→L\ell: C_1(G) \to Lℓ:C1​(G)→L, using the fact that C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) is the free commutative monoid on EEE. So this extended map

ℓ ⁣:C1(G)→L \ell \colon C_1(G) \to Lℓ:C1​(G)→L

is given by

α1e1+⋯+αnen→∑i=1kαiℓ(ei) \alpha_1 e_1 + \cdots + \alpha_n e_n \to \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i \ell(e_i) α1​e1​+⋯+αn​en​→∑i=1k​αi​ℓ(ei​)

where αi∈N\alpha_i \in \mathbb{N}αi​∈N.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:14):

Thanks. But what does the term αiℓ(ei)\alpha_i \ell(e_i)αi​ℓ(ei​) mean?

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:15):

Is it just summing l(ei)l(e_i)l(ei​) αi\alpha_iαi​ times?

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 17:19):

Yes. Every element of the free commutative monoid on a set {x,y}\{x,y\}{x,y}, for example, can be written as mx+nym x + n y mx+ny where m,n∈Nm,n \in \mathbb{N}m,n∈N.

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 17:19):

We're writing the monoid operation as addition here, which I admit is somewhat confusing because we often write it as multiplication!

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 17:20):

When doing co/homology, people write the operation in the coefficient group AAA as addition, so all the formulas will look more familiar if we do that for LLL too.

John Baez (Apr 13 2025 at 17:23):

There's a theory of modules for rigs (some people call them 'semi-modules'.) A commutative monoid is the same as a module of the rig N\mathbb{N}N. For any rig RRR I like to denote the free RRR-module on a set SSS by R[S]R[S]R[S]: it's the set of finite RRR-linear combinations of elements of SSS. So the free commutative monoid on SSS is N[E]\mathbb{N}[E]N[E]. We can add elements of this, and multiply them by natural numbers.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 13 2025 at 17:24):

Yes. Thanks I got the point.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 02:05):

@John Baez I am slowly realising (still a lot more to realise)!!! The definition you wrote about "homology monoids" is very beautiful. The fact that we are working with the free commutative monoid N[E]\mathbb{N}[E]N[E] instead of the free abelian group Z[E]\mathbb{Z}[E]Z[E] says

GGG is a directed graph and is seen as a directed space by the "non-existence of inverse" in the commutative monoid (N,+,0)(\mathbb{N}, +, 0)(N,+,0). By not using Z[E]\mathbb{Z}[E]Z[E], it is clearly telling we are not considering our directed graph as a usual non-directed topological space example.

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 05:15):

Thanks! You're exactly right. I noticed that the work we're already done on monoid-labeled directed graphs actually points to a generalization of 1st homology for directed graphs, where we use commutative monoids at every point where people usually use abelian groups.

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 05:38):

This new 'directed first homology' is interesting: you can easily find two directed graphs homeomorphic to a circle, one whose directed first homology is N\mathbb{N}N, and one whose directed first homology is 000. It just depends on whether you can go around the circle following a directed path or not!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 12:13):

John Baez said:

Thanks! You're exactly right. I noticed that the work we're already done on monoid-labeled directed graphs actually points to a generalization of 1st homology for directed graphs, where we use commutative monoids at every point where people usually use abelian groups.

Thank you. Yes, I agree.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 12:16):

John Baez said:

This new 'directed first homology' is interesting: you can easily find two directed graphs homeomorphic to a circle, one whose directed first homology is N\mathbb{N}N, and one whose directed first homology is 000. It just depends on whether you can go around the circle following a directed path or not!

Nice!! Yes, indeed!! I didn't observe this before. Thanks!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 15:51):

John Baez said:

Theorem. H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is a free commutative monoid. There is a unique set of generators ci∈H1(G)c_i \in H_1(G)ci​∈H1​(G) such that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is free on the set {ci}\{c_i\}{ci​}.

I am trying to make an attempt to find the candidate for the generators:

We know c=α1e1+α2e2+⋯αkekc= \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2+ \cdots \alpha_{k}e_{k}c=α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯αk​ek​ is a cycle iff

α1s(e1)+α2s(e2)+⋯αks(ek)=α1t(e1)+α2t(e2)+⋯αkt(ek)\alpha_1s(e_1) + \alpha_2s(e_2) + \cdots \alpha_{k}s(e_{k})= \alpha_1t(e_1) + \alpha_2t(e_2) + \cdots \alpha_{k}t(e_{k}) α1​s(e1​)+α2​s(e2​)+⋯αk​s(ek​)=α1​t(e1​)+α2​t(e2​)+⋯αk​t(ek​).......(*)

Now, I will say ccc is a minimal cycle if for any proper subset S⊆{e1,e2,⋯ ,ek}S \subseteq \lbrace e_1, e_2, \cdots, e_{k} \rbraceS⊆{e1​,e2​,⋯,ek​}, the corresponding analogous equation of (*) does not hold. [I think this also matches my intuition if I think about your basic feedback loops].

Now, let the set cyclemin={c∈H1(G) ⁣:ccycle_{min}= \lbrace c \in H_1(G) \colon ccyclemin​={c∈H1​(G):c is a minimal cycle }\rbrace}.

I claim the following:

My minimal cycles are your basic feedback loops i.e H1(G)=N[cyclemin]H_{1}(G)= \mathbb{N}[cycle_{min}]H1​(G)=N[cyclemin​].

I am trying to to show why I think so:

Let c=α1e1+α2e2+⋯αkekc= \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2+ \cdots \alpha_{k}e_{k}c=α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯αk​ek​ be an arbitrary element of H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G). Now, if ccc is a minimal then we are done. Otherwise, let us assume ccc is not minimal. Then by definition there is a proper subset S⊆{e1,e2,⋯ ,ek}S \subseteq \lbrace e_1, e_2, \cdots, e_{k} \rbraceS⊆{e1​,e2​,⋯,ek​} such that the equation (*) holds for SSS. Now, since we are working with "finite linear combinations", I think this process will end in a minimal loop after repeating this process for finitely many steps.

I think from the above, one can deduce your theorem (of course, one needs to fill in a lot of details).

I may be wrong.. I just thought about it!!

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 16:07):

I believe an approach like this should work, and I hope you can turn it into a proof.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 16:11):

Thank you. Yes, I think I can.

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 16:16):

For a while I thought there might be a quick nonconstructive proof that goes like this:

C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) is the free commutative monoid on the set of edges of the graph GGG. H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is a submonoid of C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G). Every submonoid of a free commutative monoid is a free commutative monoid. Thus H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is a free commutative monoid.

But this proof is wrong! Every subgroup of a free abelian group is a free abelian group - that's why I hoped this would work for monoids too. But then I remembered there's a whole big subject that studies submonoids of the free commutative monoid on one generator, N\mathbb{N}N. These are called numerical monoids, and most of them are not free. This one is free:

{0,3,6,9,12,… } \{0,3,6,9,12, \dots \}{0,3,6,9,12,…}

but we can throw in the number 8 and close under addition to get one that is not free:

{0,3,6,8,9,11,12,14,15,16,… } \{0, 3, 6, 8, 9, 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, \dots \}{0,3,6,8,9,11,12,14,15,16,…}

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 16:18):

So, this approach doesn't work; there seems to be something special about H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) that makes it free. And it's very good to understand your 'minimal cycles' concretely, because this is a practical subject. So even if there were a quick nonconstructive proof that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is free, we'd also want a more algorithmic proof, like the one you're trying to get.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 19:03):

John Baez said:

So, this approach doesn't work; there seems to be something special about H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) that makes it free. And it's very good to understand your 'minimal cycles' concretely, because this is a practical subject. So even if there were a quick nonconstructive proof that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is free, we'd also want a more algorithmic proof, like the one you're trying to get.

Thank you. I completely agree that "understanding of minimal cycles" would be important for practical purposes as then we can narrow down our focus to "these objects" to study motifs like feedback loops etc, and hence this in turn would make things a bit simpler. I really like your line "there seems to be something special about H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) that makes it free". I think I need some more time to understand this line in a better way.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 19:04):

John Baez said:

But then I remembered there's a whole big subject that studies submonoids of the free commutative monoid on one generator, N\mathbb{N}N. These are called numerical monoids, and most of them are not free. This one is free:

{0,3,6,9,12,… } \{0,3,6,9,12, \dots \}{0,3,6,9,12,…}

but we can throw in the number 8 and close under addition to get one that is not free:

{0,3,6,8,9,11,12,14,15,16,… } \{0, 3, 6, 8, 9, 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, \dots \}{0,3,6,8,9,11,12,14,15,16,…}

Interesting objects!! I was not aware of these objects until now. Thanks!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 14 2025 at 19:22):

@John Baez I am sharing some feelings about two things that we have already developed in our paper:

1) By Kleisli morphism approach, we can zoom in (add details) in a motif like feedback loop, and hence, often helps in finding a very complicated motif in a large network. [Complications are arising by adding extra causal relatioships in the framework.]

2) By the directed homology approach, we can express a complicated feedback loop interms of simpler loops (basic feedback loops), and hence, often helps in finding a very complicated motif in a large network. [Complications are arising by adding extra feedback loops in the framework.]

So, if I am understanding correctly, both (1) and (2) are serving a similar purpose but in different ways (at least at the moment, it seems so!!). Another good thing is that (1) needs a monoid structure in the labeling set but (2) needs a commutative monoid structure in the labelling set.. (so that the map ℓ ⁣:C1(G)→L\ell \colon C_1(G) \to Lℓ:C1​(G)→L is a homomorphism of monoids). However, I think most of the monoids that we are interested in are commutative.

In this context, what are some non-commutative monoids that may be interesting for our purpose?

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 21:02):

Those thoughts are nice, thanks.

Right now I don't know noncommutative monoids that are useful for applications of "graphs with polarities". So, right now the only reason to start by studying graphs labeled by elements of a general monoid and then turn to graphs labeled by elements of a commutative monoid is that we're mathematicians and we like to see how much we can do with the minimum amount of structure. Maybe later someone will invent some good applications of noncommutative monoids to this subject; then our paper will still be useful to them.

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 21:10):

I think of (1) and (2) as serving related purposes, and we have barely begun to explore what we can do with them.

Here's one simple relation between them:

Suppose LLL is a commutative monoid. Then for each ℓ∈L\ell \in Lℓ∈L there is an LLL-labeled graph with one vertex and one edge labeled by ℓ\ellℓ. We can call this the 'walking loop with feedback equal to ℓ\ellℓ'.

When we have any LLL-labeled graph GGG, each cycle in this graph gets a holonomy valued in LLL, which we can call its feedback. If the cycle looks like this

v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0 v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​

its feedback is defined to be

ℓ(e1)+⋯+ℓ(en) \ell(e_1) + \cdots + \ell(e_n) ℓ(e1​)+⋯+ℓ(en​)

Now for the relation: a cycle in an LLL-labeled graph GGG has feedback ℓ∈L\ell \in Lℓ∈L if and only if there is a Kleisli map from the walking loop with feedback equal to ℓ\ellℓ to (G,ℓ)(G,\ell)(G,ℓ), that maps the loop to this cycle.

John Baez (Apr 14 2025 at 21:12):

This is not very deep, but it suggests that we're starting to build a little tool kit of ideas.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 01:45):

Here is a little idea that might help you with your proof, @Adittya Chaudhuri. I believe any commutative monoid has a [[preorder]] ≤\le≤ defined as follows:

x≤y x \le yx≤y iff there exists zzz such that y=x+z y = x + zy=x+z

This is not always a partial order: for example, in an abelian group we have x≤yx \le yx≤y for all xxx and yyy.

However, for the commutative monoid H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) I can prove this is a partial order, i.e. x≤yx \le yx≤y and y≤xy \le x y≤x imply x=yx = yx=y.

Then we can define a minimal cycle in GGG to be a cycle that is minimal with respect to this partial order: more precisely, c∈H1(G)c \in H_1(G)c∈H1​(G) is minimal if for any cycle c′∈H1(G)c' \in H_1(G)c′∈H1​(G) with c′≤cc' \le cc′≤c we have d=0d = 0d=0.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 01:45):

Then we want to prove H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is free on the set of minimal cycles.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 05:52):

However, the proof of this probably won't be abstract nonsense about commutative monoids! As I mentioned before, H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) has some special features.

If you get stuck you might like to look at the proof of Lemma 4 in my paper Topological crystals. This is about undirected finite graphs, so it's a bit different. It uses homology with coefficients in Z\mathbb{Z}Z rather than N\mathbb{N}N. But some of the ideas and tricks may still be relevant!

This lemma says that any integer linear combination of edges of a graph that is a 1-cycle can be written as a finite sum of 'simple loops', meaning loops in which each vertex appears at most once.

This is quite similar to what we're thinking about now. In some ways it's more complicated, because using integer coefficients one can't use the ordering ≤\le≤ defined as above. Instead one needs to use a concept of the "support" of a cycle, which is defined at the bottom of page 10.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 06:55):

John Baez said:

Those thoughts are nice, thanks.

Right now I don't know noncommutative monoids that are useful for applications of "graphs with polarities". So, right now the only reason to start by studying graphs labeled by elements of a general monoid and then turn to graphs labeled by elements of a commutative monoid is that we're mathematicians and we like to see how much we can do with the minimum amount of structure. Maybe later someone will invent some good applications of noncommutative monoids to this subject; then our paper will still be useful to them.

Thank you!!

Yes, I fully agree to your point : we're mathematicians and we like to see how much we can do with the minimum amount of structure. Maybe later someone will invent some good applications of noncommutative monoids to this subject; then our paper will still be useful to them.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 07:00):

John Baez said:

I think of (1) and (2) as serving related purposes, and we have barely begun to explore what we can do with them.

Now for the relation: a cycle in an LLL-labeled graph GGG has feedback ℓ∈L\ell \in Lℓ∈L if and only if there is a Kleisli map from the walking loop with feedback equal to ℓ\ellℓ to (G,ℓ)(G,\ell)(G,ℓ), that maps the loop to this cycle.

As you said, it could be interesting in finding various relations between (1) and (2) and interprete their meaning for applications!! But, your result already demonstrates one in this direction!! Nice!! Thank you!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 07:03):

@John Baez Thank you so much for the ideas on the proof of the theorem. I am now trying to understand your ideas and implement them to construct a proof of your theorem by combining my previous approach.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 07:04):

John Baez said:

This is not very deep, but it suggests that we're starting to build a little tool kit of ideas.

I fully agree!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 16:11):

John Baez said:

Theorem. H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is a free commutative monoid. There is a unique set of generators ci∈H1(G)c_i \in H_1(G)ci​∈H1​(G) such that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is free on the set {ci}\{c_i\}{ci​}.

Below I am trying to write down a proof based on the ideas you provided:

Proof: Consider the preorder relation in commutative monoid H1(G)≤C1(G)H_1(G) \leq C_1(G)H1​(G)≤C1​(G) defined as x≤yx \leq yx≤y iff there exists z∈H1(G)z \in H_1(G)z∈H1​(G) such that y=x+zy=x+zy=x+z. Now, define a cycle c∈H1(G)c \in H_1(G)c∈H1​(G) a minimal cycle as a minimal element cmin∈H1(G)c_{min} \in H_1(G)cmin​∈H1​(G) with respect to ≤\leq≤. Define a set Cyclemin={c∈H1(G) ⁣:cCycle_{min}= \lbrace c \in H_{1}(G) \colon cCyclemin​={c∈H1​(G):c is a minimal cycle }\rbrace}. The fact that every element of C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) is a finite linear combination of the elements of EEE with coefficients in N\mathbb{N}N guarantees that the set CycleminCycle_{min}Cyclemin​ is non-empty. It can be observed that if x,y∈C1(G)x,y \in C_1(G)x,y∈C1​(G) and x+y=0x+y=0x+y=0, then x=0x=0x=0 and y=0y=0y=0. Now, say c≤c′c \leq c'c≤c′ and c′≤cc' \leq cc′≤c. Then, there exists d,e∈H1(G)d,e \in H_1(G)d,e∈H1​(G) such that c′=c+dc'=c+dc′=c+d and c=c′+ec=c' + ec=c′+e. Hence, we have c+c′=(c+c′)+d+ec+c'=(c+c') +d +ec+c′=(c+c′)+d+e. Because natural numbers can not be added to zero, we have d+e=0d+e=0d+e=0. Hence, by the said observation, d=0d=0d=0 and e=0e=0e=0. Hence, c=c′c=c'c=c′. Thus the preorder "≤\leq≤" in H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is actually a partial order. Thus, we have the liberty to say cminc_{min}cmin​ is a minimal cycle if it can not be written as sum of cycles or, more precisely, for any cycle c≤cminc \leq c_{min}c≤cmin​, we have cmin=cc_{min}=ccmin​=c. Thus, the definition of a minimal cycle matches our intuition. Now, we claim H1(G)=N[Cyclemin]H_1(G)=\mathbb{N}[Cycle_{min}]H1​(G)=N[Cyclemin​]. To see this we take an arbitrary cycle c∈H1(G)c \in H_{1}(G)c∈H1​(G). If ccc is minimal, we are done, otherwise, there exists c1,x1∈H1(G)c_1 , x_1 \in H_1(G)c1​,x1​∈H1​(G) such that c=c1+x1.c=c_1 + x_1.c=c1​+x1​. Now, if c1c_1c1​ and x1x_1x1​ are minimal we are done, otherwise, we repeat the process, with respect to c1c_1c1​ or/and x1x_1x1​. Since, ccc is a finite linear combination of the elements of EEE with coefficients in N\mathbb{N}N, this process will end in finite number of steps to a representation of the form c=α1cmin1+α2cmin2+⋅+αkcminkc= \alpha_1c^{1}_{min} + \alpha_2c^{2}_{min} + \cdot + \alpha_{k} c^{k}_{min}c=α1​cmin1​+α2​cmin2​+⋅+αk​cmink​, where αi,k∈N\alpha_{i},k \in \mathbb{N}αi​,k∈N and each cminic^{i}_{min}cmini​ is a minimal cycle for each i=1,2,…,ki =1,2, \ldots, k i=1,2,…,k. Hence, H1(G)=N[Cyclemin]H_1(G)=\mathbb{N}[Cycle_{min}]H1​(G)=N[Cyclemin​].

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:16):

I'm reading your proof - thanks very much for writing it up here.

Thus, we have the liberty to say cminc_{min}cmin​ is a minimal cycle if it can not be written as sum of cycles or, more precisely, for any cycle c≤cminc \leq c_{min}c≤cmin​, we have cmin=cc_{min}=ccmin​=c.

This needs a little correction, since 000 is a cycle:

Thus, we have the liberty to say cminc_{min}cmin​ is a minimal cycle if it can not be written as sum of two cycles which are both nonzero, or, more precisely, for any nonzero cycle c≤cminc \leq c_{min}c≤cmin​, we have cmin=cc_{min}=ccmin​=c.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:17):

(It's a lot like how a prime number is not a product of two natural numbers that are both >1\gt 1>1.)

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 16:18):

Thanks, yes, I understand your point.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:21):

What you've proved is that every cycle is a sum of nnn minimal cycles for some n≥0n \ge 0n≥0. (The sum of 000 minimal cycles is 000.)

You have not yet shown that every cycle can be uniquely expressed as a sum of nnn minimal cycles. That's what we need to show that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is the free commutative monoid on the set of minimal cycles, i.e.

H1(G)≅N[Cyclemin] H_1(G) \cong \mathbb{N}[\text{Cycle}_{\text{min}} ]H1​(G)≅N[Cyclemin​]

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:21):

In other words, you've shown that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is generated by the set of minimal cycles, but not yet that it's freely generated.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:23):

To show that it's freely generated we'll need to use more special features of H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G). I suspect we'll need to use tricks similar to those used in proving Lemma 4 in Topological crystals.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:24):

It's worth proving that H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is freely generated by the set of minimal cycles, because this gives a complete description of H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G), i.e. an isomorphism theorem

H1(G)≅N[Cyclemin]H_1(G) \cong \mathbb{N}[\text{Cycle}_{\text{min}} ]H1​(G)≅N[Cyclemin​]

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 16:24):

Thanks. I understand your point. Yes, I will look at the Lemma 3 in your paper.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:26):

That's good, but separately we should start thinking about how we would prove that every cycle is uniquely a sum of minimal cycles. Suppose a cycle can be written in two different ways as a sum of minimal cycles. What do we do now, to get a contradiction? I guess we first subtract off all minimal cycles that appear in both sides of the equation.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 16:28):

When we're done we get

m1c1+⋯+nkmk=n1d1+⋯+mhdh m_1 c_1 + \cdots + n_k m_k = n_1 d_1 + \cdots + m_h d_h m1​c1​+⋯+nk​mk​=n1​d1​+⋯+mh​dh​

where cic_ici​ and did_idi​ are minimal cycles and none of the cic_ici​ equal any of the djd_jdj​.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 16:28):

Yes.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 17:08):

I think at this point in the proof it may be useful to note that the free commutative monoid on n generators (Nn\mathbb{N}^nNn) has a natural injection to the free abelian group on n generators (Zn\mathbb{Z}^nZn), so our C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) becomes a submonoid of the usual C1(G,Z)C_1(G,\mathbb{Z})C1​(G,Z). This allows us to subtract, and it becomes sufficient to show your minimal cycles freely generate Z1(G,Z)Z_1(G, \mathbb{Z})Z1​(G,Z).

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 17:08):

We were here:

When we're done we get

m1c1+⋯+nkmk=n1d1+⋯+mhdh m_1 c_1 + \cdots + n_k m_k = n_1 d_1 + \cdots + m_h d_h m1​c1​+⋯+nk​mk​=n1​d1​+⋯+mh​dh​

where cic_ici​ and did_idi​ are minimal cycles and none of the cic_ici​ equal any of the djd_jdj​.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 17:12):

But now we can subtract, and we can see it's sufficient to show:

If c1,…,ckc_1,\dots,c_kc1​,…,ck​ are distinct minimal cycles and

n1c1+⋯+nkck=0 n_1 c_1 +\cdots + n_k c_k=0n1​c1​+⋯+nk​ck​=0

for some integers nin_ini​, then all thrse integers are zero.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 17:14):

John Baez said:

When we're done we get

m1c1+⋯+nkmk=n1d1+⋯+mhdh m_1 c_1 + \cdots + n_k m_k = n_1 d_1 + \cdots + m_h d_h m1​c1​+⋯+nk​mk​=n1​d1​+⋯+mh​dh​

where cic_ici​ and did_idi​ are minimal cycles and none of the cic_ici​ equal any of the djd_jdj​.

To show the above, isn't the "minimality" enough? Do we really need to go to Z\mathbb{Z}Z? I mean a minimal cycle can not be written in terms of sum of cycles. Then, how can the coeefficients(attached to same minimal cycles) differ in LHS and RHS?

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 17:15):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

Thanks. I understand your point. Yes, I will look at the Lemma 3 in your paper.

Sorry, I meant Lemma 4.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 15 2025 at 17:28):

John Baez said:

But now we can subtract, and we can see it's sufficient to show:

If c1,…,ckc_1,\dots,c_kc1​,…,ck​ are distinct minimal cycles and

n1c1+⋯+nkck=0 n_1 c_1 +\cdots + n_k c_k=0n1​c1​+⋯+nk​ck​=0

for some integers nin_ini​, then all thrse integers are zero.

I am assuming you meant the use of Z\mathbb{Z}Z in this step but not in the previous one.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 17:49):

Since the homology monoid is injectively mapped to the homology group, an equation between elements of the homology monoid holds iff the corresponding equation holds in the homology group.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 17:53):

But yes, in this equation the coefficients are natural numbers:

When we're done we get

m1c1+⋯+nkmk=n1d1+⋯+mhdh m_1 c_1 + \cdots + n_k m_k = n_1 d_1 + \cdots + m_h d_h m1​c1​+⋯+nk​mk​=n1​d1​+⋯+mh​dh​

where cic_ici​ and did_idi​ are minimal cycles and none of the cic_ici​ equal any of the djd_jdj​.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 20:27):

There's a lot more to say, but here's one thing. We've been talking about 'minimal cycles' defined in a purely algebraic way, i.e. cycles that aren't sums of other cycles in a nontrivial way. The paper Topological Crystals instead takes a more geometrical approach: it talks about 'simple loops', which are loops of edges which visit each vertex at most once.

Just by looking at examples, I believe these are the same thing. But we haven't proved this. We could try to prove it.

The advantage of the algebraic definition is that it becomes rather easy to prove every cycle is a sum of minimal cycles.

Lemma 4 in Topological Crystals works for undirected graphs, but in that context it shows that every cycle is a sum of (cycles coming from) simple loops. This takes some work! The argument is mainly due to Greg Egan. It's a good example of how we can take advantage of the graph structure and think geometrically, essentially giving an algorithm to find a simple loop hiding inside any cycle. We can then subtract off this simple loop, and we get a new cycle whose support is contained in that of the original cycle. Then we repeat, and this must eventually terminate.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 20:33):

Anyway, I believe we will need to really work and think in terms of graphs to prove this conjecture, which I foolishly called a theorem:

Conjecture. If GGG is a finite directed graph, its first homology monoid H1(G)=Z1(G)H_1(G) = Z_1(G)H1​(G)=Z1​(G) is the free commutative monoid on the set of minimal cycles (or alternatively: simple loops).

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 20:34):

That's really two conjectures, and proving either one would be fine for now, though it would be even nicer to prove that minimal cycles are the same as simple loops, since then we'd know the 'algebraic' and 'geometrical' approaches are the same!

Anyway, I think we really need to get our hands dirty and work here, more like graph theorists than category theorists.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 04:53):

(deleted)

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 04:56):

John Baez said:

That's really two conjectures, and proving either one would be fine for now, though it would be even nicer to prove that minimal cycles are the same as simple loops, since then we'd know the 'algebraic' and 'geometrical' approaches are the same!

Anyway, I think we really need to get our hands dirty and work here, more like graph theorists than category theorists.

Thank you!! Yes, I also feel that to align our "graph theoretic intuition" with our "algebraic definitions" it is necessary to see how a cycle c∈H1(G)c \in H_1(G)c∈H1​(G) is related to a graph theoretic cycle. For that, first I want to define a graph theoretic cycle of a presheaf graph G=[E⇉V]G=[E \rightrightarrows V]G=[E⇉V].

Definition:
A graph theoretic cycle of a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V) is defined as a finite sequence {xi}i=1n\lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1}{xi​}i=1n​ of edges satisfying the following properties:

t(x1)=s(x2),⋯ ,t(xn−1)=s(xn)t(x_1)=s(x_2), \cdots, t(x_{n-1})=s(x_n)t(x1​)=s(x2​),⋯,t(xn−1​)=s(xn​) and t(xn)=s(x1)t(x_n)=s(x_1)t(xn​)=s(x1​).

Definition:
Given a graph theoretic cycle {xi}i=1n\lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1}{xi​}i=1n​ of a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V), we define a graphical cycle as x1+x2+⋯+xn∈C1(G)x_1 +x_2 + \cdots + x_{n} \in C_1(G)x1​+x2​+⋯+xn​∈C1​(G).

Now, I think, to see the relation between our intuition and your homology theory, I think the first step is to prove the following lemma:

Lemma
Given a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V) and a graph theoretic cycle {xi}i=1n\lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1}{xi​}i=1n​ we have the following:

a) The graphical cycle x1+x2+⋯xnx_1 +x_2 + \cdots x_{n} x1​+x2​+⋯xn​ is an element of H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G).
b) For any element c=α1e1+α2e2+⋯+αkekc= \alpha_1e_1 + \alpha_2e_2 + \cdots + \alpha_{k}e_{k}c=α1​e1​+α2​e2​+⋯+αk​ek​ of H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G), there exists a set of graph theoretic cycles {xi}i=1n\lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1}{xi​}i=1n​ such that each xix_{i}xi​ is an element of the set {e1,e2,…,ek}\lbrace e_1,e_2,\ldots ,e_{k} \rbrace{e1​,e2​,…,ek​}.

(a) follows from t(x1)=s(x2),⋯ ,t(xn−1)=s(xn)t(x_1)=s(x_2), \cdots, t(x_{n-1})=s(x_n)t(x1​)=s(x2​),⋯,t(xn−1​)=s(xn​) and t(xn)=s(x1)t(x_n)=s(x_1)t(xn​)=s(x1​).

Next we define a set Cyclegr:={{xi}i=1n ⁣:{xi}i=1nCycle_{gr}:= \lbrace \lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1} \colon \lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1}Cyclegr​:={{xi​}i=1n​:{xi​}i=1n​ is graph theoretic cycle }\rbrace}. Then, (a) allows us to define a function F ⁣:Cyclegr→H1(G)\mathcal{F} \colon Cycle_{gr} \to H_1(G) F:Cyclegr​→H1​(G), {xi}i=1n↦x1+x2+⋯+xn \lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1} \mapsto x_1 +x_2 + \cdots+ x_{n} {xi​}i=1n​↦x1​+x2​+⋯+xn​. Since H1(G)H_{1}(G)H1​(G) is a submonoid of the free commutative monoid C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G), the function F\mathcal{F}F is injective.

However, it feels "if your definition of homology is correct", then proving (b) is like "extracting/characterising the information of the space" from the/in terms of the "information of homology groups of the space", about which I am not sure how much information we can recover if we do not talk on "homotopy in our graphs" *. I am not sure!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 05:16):

Next, may be we can define a simple loop (from the way you defined) in terms of our new language as follows:

Definition:

Given a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V), a simple loop is a graphical cycle x1+x2+⋯+xnx_1 +x_2 + \cdots +x_{n} x1​+x2​+⋯+xn​ induced from a graph theoretic cycle {xi}i=1n \lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1}{xi​}i=1n​, such that s(xi)≠t(xj)s(x_i) \neq t(x_{j})s(xi​)=t(xj​) for all i,ji,ji,j except (i,j)=(1,n)(i,j)=(1,n)(i,j)=(1,n).

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 05:24):

@John Baez Now, I think to show that you have proposed only 1 conjecture but not two, we may need to prove the following Proposition (via the correspondence described above):

Proposition:

Given a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V), there is a bijection between the set of simple loops and minimal cycles.

Or, may be a (weaker version)

Given a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V), there is an injective function from the the set of simple loops to the set of minimal cycles.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 08:26):

John Baez said:

Conjecture. If GGG is a finite directed graph, its first homology monoid H1(G)=Z1(G)H_1(G) = Z_1(G)H1​(G)=Z1​(G) is the free commutative monoid on the set of minimal cycles (or alternatively: simple loops).

@Morgan Rogers (he/him) produced (possibly) a counter-example here #theory: mathematics > Submonoids of free commutative monoids @ 💬

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 10:44):

An observation:

Using the function F ⁣:Cyclegr→H1(G)\mathcal{F} \colon Cycle_{gr} \to H_1(G) F:Cyclegr​→H1​(G), {xi}i=1n↦x1+x2+⋯+xn \lbrace x_{i} \rbrace^{n}_{i=1} \mapsto x_1 +x_2 + \cdots+ x_{n} {xi​}i=1n​↦x1​+x2​+⋯+xn​, I think we may talk about topological ordering in terms of your directed homology monoids as follows:

  • For a directed graph X⊆V×VX \subseteq V \times VX⊆V×V, if H1(X)H_1(X)H1​(X) is trivial, then XXX can be topologically ordered.

The above statement makes sense because every directed graph can be seen as a graph of our sense (presheaf graph).

John Baez (Apr 16 2025 at 17:30):

That sounds right. People like to study DAGs, or directed acyclic graphs, and these have trivial first homology monoid. A DAG is good for when you have a bunch of tasks that needs to be done, and a→ba \to ba→b means you have to do task aaa before you do task bbb.

John Baez (Apr 16 2025 at 17:32):

The theorem you mention says that for a DAG there's some way you can order the tasks so that you can do them in that order.

John Baez (Apr 16 2025 at 17:33):

I'm not very interested in DAGs right now because I'm interested in 'causal loop diagrams' and their generalizations, where the focus is on feedback loops, i.e. directed cycles.

John Baez (Apr 16 2025 at 17:34):

I think I see how to prove this:

Proposition:

Given a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V), there is a bijection between the set of simple loops and minimal cycles.

and I think it's still somewhat interesting even though my conjecture was false.

John Baez (Apr 16 2025 at 17:41):

I'm not quite sure how the paper should go. Maybe we should define the first homology monoid, show that it's generated by minimal cycles (your result), and then prove these correspond to simple loops (I hope I can do this). We can include an example of a graph whose first homology monoid is not freely generated by the minimal cycles.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 18:33):

John Baez said:

I'm not very interested in DAGs right now because I'm interested in 'causal loop diagrams' and their generalizations, where the focus is on feedback loops, i.e. directed cycles.

Yes, I understand your point. Technically, as you explained, we are mostly interested when H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is not trivial. However, Topological ordering happens only when H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) is trivial.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 18:37):

John Baez said:

I think I see how to prove this:

Proposition:

Given a graph G:=((E,V),s,t ⁣:E→V)G:= ((E,V), s,t \colon E \to V)G:=((E,V),s,t:E→V), there is a bijection between the set of simple loops and minimal cycles.

and I think it's still somewhat interesting even though my conjecture was false.

Yes, I think then, we can relate the ideas of our paper with your paper on Topological crystals. Also, I think, as a result, it would be easier for us to imagine minimal cycles concretely.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 16 2025 at 18:51):

John Baez said:

I'm not quite sure how the paper should go. Maybe we should define the first homology monoid, show that it's generated by minimal cycles (your result), and then prove these correspond to simple loops (I hope I can do this). We can include an example of a graph whose first homology monoid is not freely generated by the minimal cycles.

Sounds good.

Since you said earlier that our paper can also be seen as an exploration of directed homology and homotopy of directed multigraphs in degree 1, I was thinking whether we can say something about the homotopy invariance of your directed 1st homology monoid (because this is true in the usual case). Since you said, the definition of a [[directed topological space]] is still not completely standard as there are multiple contenders, I was thinking about the following:

Step 1:
Defining a suitable (useful for practical purposes) notion of directed homotopy equivalence between presheaf graphs.

Step 2:
To show that your notion of directed homology monoid of a presheaf graph is invariant under directed homotopy equivalence between presheaf graphs.

Maybe I am not making much sense. But, today, I was thinking about these.

John Baez (Apr 16 2025 at 19:07):

I'd like to focus on developing tools that will be useful for our applications. I think we agreed that the end of the paper will explain how our work is useful in:

  • systems biology - regulatory networks
  • 'systems dynamics' - epidemiology, business dynamics and industrial dynamics

In all of these we can find and explain good examples of

  • commutative monoids used for describing polarities
  • motifs
  • the analysis of feedback loops

I think this will be quite nice. The homotopy theory of directed graphs sounds like it should be in a different paper - a paper published in a journal that's good for homotopy theory, not Compositionality. A good paper tells a clear story, so it shouldn't consist of "everything we happened to have thought about so far".

It would be cool to study the homotopy theory of directed graphs... but you're trying to get a job in math applied to systems biology, right? If so, it pays to be strategic about the papers you write.

  • 'systems dynamics' - epidemiology, business dynamics and industrial dynamics

  • commutative monoids used for describing polarities
  • motifs

Yes, I completely agree. It is also telling a nice and clear story.

John Baez (Apr 16 2025 at 20:21):

Great! One good thing about this philosophy is that it lets you build up a supply of ideas for papers which you can write later. That way, you'll eventually have a big list of papers you can write, and whenever you want to write a paper you can choose the best one - where 'best' might mean most fun, or best for getting your next job, or whatever you want.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 17 2025 at 02:47):

John Baez said:

I'm not quite sure how the paper should go. Maybe we should define the first homology monoid, show that it's generated by minimal cycles (your result), and then prove these correspond to simple loops (I hope I can do this). We can include an example of a graph whose first homology monoid is not freely generated by the minimal cycles.

Now, that we know that your homology monoid of a directed graph may not be free, I was wondering (from the point of applications) that if it is possible to systematically characterise the graphs GGG whose homology monoids are free on the minimal cycles.

I am trying to explain below why I said "from the point of applications"

Say we encounter a very big causal loop diagram (provided by domain specific scientists like biologists, policy makers, epidemeologists, ..etc), and we want to study its feedback loops. Then, according to the theory we developed, we may first look at the minimal cycles. Now, if from the nature of the causal loop diagram (by using the above-mentioned-proposed-characterisation/finding conditions) we know from the begining that the homology monoid of the underlying directed graph of such a diagram is free on the set of minimal cycles, then we may be able to completely characterise our study of feedback loops in that diagram by focussing only on its minimal cycles.

James Deikun (Apr 17 2025 at 03:51):

I think the situation in the reduced counterexample is really the only "forbidden minor", insofar as that concept even applies to this kind of graph. A theta graph for example is fine, you have to have at least two points of intersection in two cycles and there has to be separation on both sides. Coming up with a meaningful way to count or label the violations is harder. Every example I can think of with three cycles reduces to one with two cycles in at least one way, just as a double check. To prove it you might want to formulate some kind of exchange principle, or a way to break minimal cycles into "subatomic particles" that freely generate a minimal free monoid containing the homology monoid.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 17 2025 at 05:05):

James Deikun said:

I think the situation in the reduced counterexample is really the only "forbidden minor", insofar as that concept even applies to this kind of graph. A theta graph for example is fine, you have to have at least two points of intersection in two cycles and there has to be separation on both sides. Coming up with a meaningful way to count or label the violations is harder. Every example I can think of with three cycles reduces to one with two cycles in at least one way, just as a double check. To prove it you might want to formulate some kind of exchange principle, or a way to break minimal cycles into "subatomic particles" that freely generate a minimal free monoid containing the homology monoid.

Thank you. I am trying to understand your ideas.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 00:57):

Let me try to prove there's a bijective correspondence between simple loops and minimal cycles.

Recall the framework. A graph GGG is a set VVV of vertices, a set EEE of edges and source and target maps s,t:E→Vs, t: E \to Vs,t:E→V. We're working with a homology monoid, so

  • the monoid of 0-chains, C0(G)C_0(G)C0​(G), is the free commutative monoid on VVV,
  • the monoid of 1-chains, C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G), is the free commutative monoid on EEE.

The source and target maps extend to monoid homomorphisms which we give the same names:

s,t:C1(G)→C0(G) s, t : C_1(G) \to C_0(G) s,t:C1​(G)→C0​(G)

We define a 1-cycle to be a 1-chain ccc with

s(c)=t(c) s(c) = t(c) s(c)=t(c)

We denote the commutative monoid of 1-cycles by Z1(G)Z_1(G)Z1​(G). We define the first homology H1(G)H_1(G)H1​(G) to be Z1(G)Z_1(G)Z1​(G) (since in general the first homology consists of 1-cycles mod 1-boundaries, but here the only 1-boundary is zero).

Later I will want to define the zeroth homology, but this is getting long so let's skip it for now.

There's a preorder on any commutative monoid given by

x≤y x \le y x≤y iff y=x+zy = x + zy=x+z for some zzz

Puzzle. Prove that for C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) this preorder is a partial order.

Puzzle. As a subset of C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G), Z1(G)Z_1(G)Z1​(G) inherits a partial order. Prove this is the same the preorder it gets by treating it as a commutative monoid in its own right. In other words, given x,y∈Z1(G)x, y \in Z_1(G)x,y∈Z1​(G) with

y=x+zy = x + zy=x+z for some z∈C1(G)z \in C_1(G)z∈C1​(G)

show that in fact we can take z∈Z1(G)z \in Z_1(G)z∈Z1​(G).

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 00:58):

We say that a 1-cycle ccc is minimal if the only 1-cycle ≤c\le c≤c is zero.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 01:04):

We define an edge path in GGG to be a finite list of edges such that the target of each edge is the source of the next. In pictures:

v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​

where we allow the degenerate case n=0n = 0n=0. Any edge path γ\gammaγ gives a 1-chain called [γ][\gamma][γ], namely the sum of its edges:

[γ]=e1+⋯+en∈C1(G) [\gamma] = e_1 + \cdots + e_n \in C_1(G)[γ]=e1​+⋯+en​∈C1​(G)

We say an edge path is a loop if vn=v0v_n = v_0vn​=v0​.

Puzzle. Show that an edge path γ\gammaγ is a loop if and only if [γ][\gamma][γ] is a 1-cycle.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 01:08):

We say a loop is simple if it visits each vertex just once, except that it ends where it starts. More precisely,

v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0 v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​

is a simple loop if all the vertices v1,…,vnv_1, \dots, v_nv1​,…,vn​ are distinct.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 01:14):

Claim 1. If γ\gammaγ is a simple loop then [γ]∈Z1(G)[\gamma] \in Z_1(G)[γ]∈Z1​(G) is a minimal cycle.

Claim 2. For each minimal cycle ccc there exists a simple loop γ\gammaγ such that [γ]=c[\gamma] = c[γ]=c.

Claim 3. If γ,γ′\gamma, \gamma'γ,γ′ are two simple loops with [γ]=[γ′][\gamma] = [\gamma'][γ]=[γ′], they differ only by where they start. That is, if γ\gammaγ is of the form

v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​

then γ′\gamma'γ′ must be of the form

vk→ek+1vk+1→ek+2⋯→envn→e1v1→e2⋯→ekvk v_k \xrightarrow{e_{k+1}} v_{k+1} \xrightarrow{e_{k+2}} \cdots \stackrel{e_n}{\to} v_n \stackrel{e_1}{\to} v_1 \stackrel{e_2}{\to} \cdots \stackrel{e_k}{\to} v_k vk​ek+1​​vk+1​ek+2​​⋯→en​vn​→e1​v1​→e2​⋯→ek​vk​

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 01:17):

So, we get a bijection between minimal cycles and equivalence classes of simple loops, where two simple loops are equivalent if they differ only by where they start!

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 01:29):

Let's start with Claim 1, which I believe is the easiest.

Proof of Claim 1. Suppose γ\gammaγ is a simple loop, say

v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​

The corresponding cycle is

[γ]=∑i=1nei [\gamma] = \sum_{i=1}^n e_i [γ]=∑i=1n​ei​

Any cycle ≤c\le c≤c must be a chain ≤c\le c≤c, and since C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G) is free on the set of edges any chain c′≤cc' \le cc′≤c must be of the form

c′=∑i∈Sei c' = \sum_{i \in S} e_i c′=∑i∈S​ei​

where S⊆{1,…,n}S \subseteq \{1, \dots, n \} S⊆{1,…,n}. We have

s(c′)=∑i∈Svi−1 s(c') = \sum_{i \in S} v_{i-1} s(c′)=∑i∈S​vi−1​

and

t(c′)=∑i∈Svi t(c') = \sum_{i \in S} v_i t(c′)=∑i∈S​vi​

If c′c'c′ is a cycle we must have s(c′)=t(c′)s(c') = t(c')s(c′)=t(c′).

But since all the vertices v1,…,vnv_1, \dots , v_nv1​,…,vn​ are distinct (while v0=vnv_0 = v_nv0​=vn​), the two sums above can only be equal if SSS is all of {1,…,n}\{1, \dots, n\}{1,…,n}, in which case c′=cc' = cc′=c, or SSS is empty, in which case c′=0c' = 0c′=0. (Note that since C0(G)C_0(G)C0​(G) is free on the set of vertices, the two sums can only be equal if they are 'visibly' equal - there are no extra relations.) Thus ccc is minimal. :black_large_square:

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 01:53):

Now let me try Claim 2, which is where things get interesting.

Claim 2. For each minimal cycle ccc there exists a simple loop γ\gammaγ such that [γ]=c[\gamma] = c[γ]=c.

Proof. Note that C1(G)C_1(G)C1​(G), the free commutative monoid on the set EEE of edges of our graph, can also be thought of as the collection of [[multisets]] of elements of EEE. This will be useful in what follows.

Let ccc be a minimal cycle. Think of it as a multiset of edges as above. It's nonempty since c=0c = 0c=0, so choose an edge in this multiset and call it v0→e1v1 v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1v0​e1​​v1​.

Now there are two cases:

  1. If v1=v0v_1 = v_0v1​=v0​ this path consisting of a single edge is a simple loop γ\gammaγ, so it gives a nonzero cycle [γ]=e1[\gamma] = e_1[γ]=e1​, so by minimality of ccc we must have c=[γ]c = [\gamma]c=[γ] and we are done.

  2. If v1≠v0v_1 \ne v_0v1​=v0​ then e1e_1e1​ is not a cycle so ccc must be a sum of e1e_1e1​ and one or more edges in the multiset c−e1c - e_1c−e1​, and at least one of these edges must have source v1v_1v1​, since otherwise it would be impossible to have s(c)=t(c)s(c) = t(c)s(c)=t(c). Choose one such edge and call it v1→e2v2 v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} v_2 v1​e2​​v2​.

In the second case we get a path

v0→e1v1→e2v2 v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} v_2 v0​e1​​v1​e2​​v2​

Now there are three cases:

  1. If v2=v0v_2 = v_0v2​=v0​ then this path is a simple loop γ\gammaγ, so it gives a nonzero cycle [γ][\gamma][γ], so by minimality of ccc we must have c=[γ]c = [\gamma]c=[γ] and we are done.

  2. If v2=v1v_2 = v_1v2​=v1​ then the path v1→e2v2 v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} v_2 v1​e2​​v2​ is a simple loop γ\gammaγ, so it gives a nonzero cycle [γ][\gamma][γ], so by minimality of ccc we must have c=[γ]c = [\gamma]c=[γ] and we are done.

  3. If v0,v1,v2v_0, v_1, v_2v0​,v1​,v2​ are distinct then e1+e2e_1 + e_2e1​+e2​ is not a cycle, so ccc must be a sum of e1+e2e_1 + e_2e1​+e2​ and one or more edges in the multiset c−e1−e2c - e_1 - e_2c−e1​−e2​, and at least one of these edges must have source v2v_2v2​, since otherwise it would be impossible to have s(c)=t(c)s(c) = t(c)s(c)=t(c). Choose one such edge and call it v2→e3v3 v_2 \xrightarrow{e_3} v_3 v2​e3​​v3​.

And so on. I could write this more formally, but I hope the pattern is clear.

Since ccc is a finite multiset of edges this process must eventually terminate: i.e., eventually the vertex vnv_nvn​ must equal one of the earlier vertices v1,…,vn−1v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}v1​,…,vn−1​. If it equals vkv_kvk​, then

vk→ek+1vk+1→ek+2⋯→envn v_k \xrightarrow{e_{k+1}} v_{k+1} \xrightarrow{e_{k+2}} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n vk​ek+1​​vk+1​ek+2​​⋯en​​vn​

is a simple loop, so

ek+1+⋯+en e_{k+1} + \cdots + e_n ek+1​+⋯+en​

is a nonzero cycle, and by minimality of ccc this must equal ccc.

Thus, we have found a simple loop γ\gammaγ with [γ]=c[\gamma] = c[γ]=c. :black_large_square:

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 05:42):

Thank you!! I am now trying to understand your proof.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 06:04):

Feel free to ask questions. Claim 2 is the hard one. In our paper I would write up a more formal inductive proof; here I am outlining the first few steps of the induction and hoping the pattern becomes clear.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 06:06):

You'll notice that the proof of Claim 2 is closely related to the proof of Lemma 4 in Topological Crystals, but it's simpler.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 06:07):

John Baez said:

Feel free to ask questions. Claim 2 is the hard one. In our paper I would write up a more formal inductive proof; here I am outlining the first few steps of the induction and hoping the pattern becomes clear.

Thank you so much. I am reading your proof.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 06:36):

The proof is really an algorithm; it may be helpful to draw a graph and a minimal cycle in this graph, and carry out the algorithm.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 11:52):

Thank you!! I think I understod the proof. Although the proof of claim 2 is more complex than the proof of claim 1 I find both the proof beautiful and interesting.

I feel the current definition of a minimal cycle that you wrote: "We say that a 1-cycle ccc is minimal if the only 1-cycle ≤c\le c≤c is zero" is a more workable definition if we look at the proof structure of your claim 2.

Regarding claim 1:

In the proof of claim 1, I like how both the facts C1(G)=N[E]C_{1}(G)= \mathbb{N}[E]C1​(G)=N[E] and c′≤[γ=v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0]c' \leq [\gamma= v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0]c′≤[γ=v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​] are together needed for writing c′=∑i∈Sei c' = \sum_{i \in S} e_i c′=∑i∈S​ei​, where S⊆{1,…,n}S \subseteq \{1, \dots, n \} S⊆{1,…,n}, which I think is crucial in the proof of claim 1.

Regarding claim 2:
I like how at every step it is using the minimality of ccc to check whether to move to the next step or not and usuing the cyclicity s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c)s(c)=t(c) to ensure that we can indeed move to the next step in a legitimate way, and finally seeing N[E]\mathbb{N}[E]N[E] as a finite multiset is ensuring that this algorithm works as it will end in a finite number of steps to our desired simple loop γ\gammaγ such that [γ]=c[\gamma]=c[γ]=c.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 11:58):

I think the proof of claim 3 shoud directly follow from the fact C1(G)=N[E]C_1(G)=\mathbb{N}[E]C1​(G)=N[E] and the defnition of [γ][\gamma][γ] when γ=v0→e1v1→e2⋯→envn=v0\gamma=v_0 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1 \xrightarrow{e_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{e_n} v_n = v_0γ=v0​e1​​v1​e2​​⋯en​​vn​=v0​.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 12:32):

Now, I think using your claim 3, it is possible to define an equivalence relation on Sloops(G)S_{loops}(G)Sloops​(G), the set of simple loops in GGG, and then we can denote the quotient set by Sloops(G)/∼S_{loops}(G)/ \simSloops​(G)/∼. Now, if we denote the set of all minimal cycles of GGG by Cyclemin(G)Cycle_{min}(G)Cyclemin​(G), then your claim 1, claim 2 and claim 3 ensure a bijection F ⁣:(Sloops(G)/∼)→Cyclemin(G)\mathcal{F} \colon (S_{loops}(G)/ \sim) \to Cycle_{min}(G)F:(Sloops​(G)/∼)→Cyclemin​(G) defined as γˉ→[γ]\bar{\gamma} \to [\gamma]γˉ​→[γ], where γˉ\bar{\gamma}γˉ​ is the equivalence class of γ\gammaγ.

Then, I think F\mathcal{F} F ensures an unambiguous way of imagining minimal cycles both from the point of graph theory and from the point of homology theory of directed topological spaces.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 13:56):

Right! We get a bijection F\mathcal{F}F and thus a precise link between the graph-theoretic and the homological way of thinking about minimal cycles.

The ability to work with minimal cycles in both ways should be important. I think it makes precise something that "systems dynamics" researchers already intuit in a rough way (though most are blissfully ignorant of homology theory).

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 14:16):

I will write up this stuff in our paper.

As we discussed in our private chat, I think our next big step is to figure out how cycles behave when we glue together open graphs, with the help of the Mayer-Vietoris exact sequence.

An open graph can be seen as a cospan of graphs

A1→i1B←i2A2 A_1 \xrightarrow{i_1} B \xleftarrow{i_2} A_2 A1​i1​​Bi2​​A2​

where AAA and BBB are graphs with no edges, just vertices.

(As you know, we can apply the theory of structured cospans whenever we have a left adjoint functor between cocomplete categories. Here this functor is the 'free graph on a set' functor Set\mathsf{Set}Set to Gph\mathsf{Gph}Gph, sending each set to a graph with that set of vertices and no edges.)

To apply Mayer-Vietoris it seems easier to assume i1i_1i1​ and i2i_2i2​ are monic. The case where they are not monic can be very important, since sometimes we want to glue together two vertices of the same graph when composing two open graphs. But temporarily I'd like to avoid thinking about it, since then we can think of i1i_1i1​ and i2i_2i2​ are giving inclusions of subspaces: geometrically realizing our graphs to get spaces, we get a cospan of spaces

∣A1∣→∣i1∣∣B∣←∣i2∣∣A2∣ |A_1| \xrightarrow{|i_1|} |B| \xleftarrow{|i_2|} |A_2| ∣A1​∣∣i1​∣​∣B∣∣i2​∣​∣A2​∣

and these are 'nice' inclusions of the sort required for Mayer-Vietoris to apply.

(Recall: the simplest version of Mayer-Vietoris applies when we have an open subspace of a topological space, but a more general version applies whenever we have a closed subspace that is a [[neighborhood retract Whenever we have a graph, viewed as a topological space, any set of vertices in that graph is a closed subspace that's a neighborhood retract.)

Now, you may wonder why I'm starting to talk about topology and a theorem about the singular homology groups of topological spaces, when we are really doing graph theory and studying the directed homology monoids of a graph!

The reason is purely efficiency: we may be able to more quickly guess what's going on if we use existing results from topology, rather than invent our own Mayer-Vietoris theorem for the directed homology monoids of a graph. In the longer run (like tomorrow) maybe we should invent our own Mayer-Vietoris theorem. But first let's see what we can do with the standard one.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 14:52):

As you know, when we compose open graphs

A1→i1B←i2A2A_1 \xrightarrow{i_1} B \xleftarrow{i_2} A_2A1​i1​​Bi2​​A2​

and

A2→i3B′←i4A3A_2 \xrightarrow{i_3} B' \xleftarrow{i_4} A_3A2​i3​​B′i4​​A3​

the key step is to take the pushout of the diagram

B←i2A2→i3B′ B \xleftarrow{i_2} A_2 \xrightarrow{i_3} B'Bi2​​A2​i3​​B′

Here we are gluing together two graphs BBB and B′B'B′ along A2A_2A2​, which is a graph consisting of just vertices.

Let's assume for now that i2i_2i2​ and i3i_3i3​ are monic. Then Mayer-Vietoris becomes relevant! Taking geometric realizations we get

∣B∣←∣i2∣∣A2∣→i3∣B′∣ |B| \xleftarrow{|i_2|} |A_2| \xrightarrow{i_3} |B'|∣B∣∣i2​∣​∣A2​∣i3​​∣B′∣

and we can think of ∣B∣|B|∣B∣ and ∣B′∣|B'|∣B′∣ as two spaces whose intersection ∣B∣∩∣B′∣|B| \cap |B'|∣B∣∩∣B′∣ is the discrete set ∣A2∣|A_2|∣A2​∣. The pushout of this diagram can thus be identified with ∣B∣∪∣B′∣|B| \cup |B'|∣B∣∪∣B′∣.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 14:59):

My notation is getting unwieldy here so let's write

X=∣B∣ X = |B| X=∣B∣
Y=∣B′∣ Y = |B'|Y=∣B′∣

We then get the Mayer-Vietoris exact sequence:

H1(X∩Y)→H1(X)⊕H1(Y)→H1(X∪Y)→∂ H_1(X \cap Y) \to H_1(X) \oplus H_1(Y) \to H_1(X \cup Y) \xrightarrow{\partial} H1​(X∩Y)→H1​(X)⊕H1​(Y)→H1​(X∪Y)∂​
H0(X∩Y)→H0(X)⊕H0(Y)→H0(X∪Y) H_0(X \cap Y) \to H_0(X) \oplus H_0(Y) \to H_0(X \cup Y) H0​(X∩Y)→H0​(X)⊕H0​(Y)→H0​(X∪Y)

where ∂\partial∂ is the famous 'boundary' map, but since X∩YX \cap YX∩Y is a discrete space this becomes

0→H1(X)⊕H1(Y)→H1(X∪Y)→∂ 0 \to H_1(X) \oplus H_1(Y) \to H_1(X \cup Y) \xrightarrow{\partial} 0→H1​(X)⊕H1​(Y)→H1​(X∪Y)∂​
H0(X∩Y)→H0(X)⊕H0(Y)→H0(X∪Y) H_0(X \cap Y) \to H_0(X) \oplus H_0(Y) \to H_0(X \cup Y) H0​(X∩Y)→H0​(X)⊕H0​(Y)→H0​(X∪Y)

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 15:03):

My main interest is in 'emergent feedback loops': how new 1-cycles can appear when we glue together two graphs, which weren't present in either graph. So I'm interested in how the map

H1(X)⊕H1(Y)→H1(X∪Y) H_1(X) \oplus H_1(Y) \to H_1(X \cup Y) H1​(X)⊕H1​(Y)→H1​(X∪Y)

can fail to be an isomorphism! And we see this happens precisely when the map ∂\partial∂ fails to equal zero.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 18:33):

Thanks!! These ideas look extremely interesting!! Although I may need more time to realise more about these. I had always been excited about these "emergent feedback loops" that can appear after gluing causal loop diagrams. But I never imagined that the homology theory could play such an interesting and powerful role in identifying them until today. It is really exciting !! Thanks a lot!!! I am learning many things!!

emergentfeedbackloop.PNG
For example, If we glue a branch graph (red) and an incoherent feedforward loop(blue) along the graph {u,v}\lbrace u,v \rbrace{u,v} with no edges, then we get a positive feedback loop and a negative feedback loop in the pushout graph. In fact, the pushout graph is itself has a special name called overlapping feedforward loop in the biochemical reaction network literature (see table 2 (number 20) of Functional Motifs in Biochemical Reaction Networks. In the attached file, both H1(B)H_1(B)H1​(B) and H1(B′)H_1(B')H1​(B′) are trivial but interestingly, H1(B∪B′)H_1(B \cup B')H1​(B∪B′) is not!!..So, the boundary map ∂\partial∂ must not be 0 as you explained at the end. However, I think, in this particular case, one may also say H1(B)⊕H1(B′)→H1(B∪B′)H_1(B) \oplus H_1(B') \to H_1(B \cup B')H1​(B)⊕H1​(B′)→H1​(B∪B′) is not an isomorphism from the fact that H1(B)H_1(B)H1​(B) and H1(B′)H_1(B')H1​(B′) are trivial but H1(B∪B′)H_1(B \cup B')H1​(B∪B′) is not.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 18:45):

John Baez said:

Now, you may wonder why I'm starting to talk about topology and a theorem about the singular homology groups of topological spaces, when we are really doing graph theory and studying the directed homology monoids of a graph!

The reason is purely efficiency: we may be able to more quickly guess what's going on if we use existing results from topology, rather than invent our own Mayer-Vietoris theorem for the directed homology monoids of a graph. In the longer run (like tomorrow) maybe we should invent our own Mayer-Vietoris theorem. But first let's see what we can do with the standard one.

Thanks!! I fully understand and agree to your point. By using the standard Mayer-Vietoris on the "geometric realisation" version, now it is clear at least "what we need to achieve" in an appropriate way!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 18:53):

Thanks again for these beautiful ideas!! I am now trying to see how we can use these ideas to construct its analogue for our case (homology monoids of directed graphs).

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 18:55):

John Baez said:

Right! We get a bijection F\mathcal{F}F and thus a precise link between the graph-theoretic and the homological way of thinking about minimal cycles.

The ability to work with minimal cycles in both ways should be important. I think it makes precise something that "systems dynamics" researchers already intuit in a rough way (though most are blissfully ignorant of homology theory).

Thanks.. Yes, I fully agree!!

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 19:48):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

emergentfeedbackloop.PNG
For example, If we glue a branch graph (red) and an incoherent feedforward loop(blue) along the graph {u,v}\lbrace u,v \rbrace{u,v} with no edges, then we get a positive feedback loop and a negative feedback loop in the pushout graph.

We don't get a feedback loop in this picture, because the pushout graph doesn't have a directed loop (which is the kind of loop my theorems are about): you can't walk around any loop while following the direction of the arrows.

But if you turn around the edge from www to vvv, making it into an edge from vvv to www, then the pushout graph does have a directed loop. In fact it has two.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 19:49):

Yes, of course..Sorry!! I meant ww w to vvv.. I have drawn wrongly!!

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 19:51):

Okay, no problem. Of course the usual homology group H1(G,Z)H_1(G,\mathbb{Z})H1​(G,Z) doesn't care which way the edges point, so your example is already good if we are studying that.

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 19:52):

By the way, I think in the paper I'll call our new homology monoids Hi(G,N)H_i(G,\mathbb{N})Hi​(G,N), and the old homology groups Hi(G,Z)H_i(G,\mathbb{Z})Hi​(G,Z). We can define Hi(G,L)H_i(G,L)Hi​(G,L) for any commutative monoid LLL.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 19:52):

Yes!! Thanks. I understand your point. I was actually trying with some of the pictures I had drawn yesterday for example 2.10.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 19:56):

Another thing , I was thinking "can we use the notation H⃗1(G,N)\vec{H}_1(G, \mathbb{N})H1​(G,N) instead of H1(G,N)H_1(G, \mathbb{N})H1​(G,N)"? In that way, we may emphasise that we consider our directed graphs as directed topological spaces but not the usual topological spaces.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 19:59):

In fact, you were initially using this notation before as here #theory: applied category theory > Graphs with polarities @ 💬

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 20:23):

I am trying to sketch an approach (based on the ideas you stated) for extending to case of homology monoids of directed graphs:

1) For any morphism ϕ ⁣:G→G′\phi \colon G \to G'ϕ:G→G′ of graphs I think there is an induced morphism ϕ∗ ⁣:H1(G,N)→H1(G′,N)\phi_{*} \colon H_1(G,\mathbb{N}) \to H_1(G', \mathbb{N})ϕ∗​:H1​(G,N)→H1​(G′,N).

2) Now, we have structured cospans L(A)→iG←oL(B)L(A) \xrightarrow{i} G \xleftarrow{o} L(B)L(A)i​Go​L(B) and L(B)→i′G′←o′L(C)L(B) \xrightarrow{i'} G' \xleftarrow{o'} L(C)L(B)i′​G′o′​L(C). Now, we can compose it to get another structured cospan L(A)→pr1∘iG+o,L(B),i′G′←pr2∘o′L(C)L(A) \xrightarrow{pr_1 \circ i} G +_{o,L(B),i'} G' \xleftarrow{pr_2 \circ o'} L(C)L(A)pr1​∘i​G+o,L(B),i′​G′pr2​∘o′​L(C), where pr1pr_1pr1​ and pr2pr_2pr2​ are natural projection maps to the pushout graph. Now, we apply (1) on these cospans to get induced maps on the homology monoids. Then, I think, we may be able to construct morphisms of commutative monoids H1(L(B),N)→H1(G,N)⊕H1(G′,N)→H1(G+o,L(B),i′G′,N) H_1(L(B), \mathbb{N}) \to H_1(G, \mathbb{N}) \oplus H_1(G', \mathbb{N}) \to H_1(G +_{o,L(B),i'} G', \mathbb{N})H1​(L(B),N)→H1​(G,N)⊕H1​(G′,N)→H1​(G+o,L(B),i′​G′,N) which becomes 0→H1(G,N)⊕H1(G′,N)→H1(G+o,L(B),i′G′,N)0 \to H_1(G, \mathbb{N}) \oplus H_1(G', \mathbb{N}) \to H_1(G +_{o,L(B),i'} G', \mathbb{N})0→H1​(G,N)⊕H1​(G′,N)→H1​(G+o,L(B),i′​G′,N).

3) Construction of boundary map δ ⁣:H1(G+o,L(B),i′G′,N)→H0(L(B),N)\delta \colon H_1(G +_{o,L(B),i'} G', \mathbb{N}) \to H_0(L(B), \mathbb{N})δ:H1​(G+o,L(B),i′​G′,N)→H0​(L(B),N).

Maybe I am misunderstanding something !! I will think about these more!!

John Baez (Apr 18 2025 at 20:34):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

Another thing , I was thinking "can we use the notation H⃗1(G,N)\vec{H}_1(G, \mathbb{N})H1​(G,N) instead of H1(G,N)H_1(G, \mathbb{N})H1​(G,N)"? In that way, we may emphasise that we consider our directed graphs as directed topological spaces but not the usual topological spaces.

I'm thinking a bit differently now: for a graph we can define what you're calling H⃗1(G,L)\vec{H}_1(G,L)H1​(G,L) for any commutative monoid LLL, but when LLL is an abeilan group this is isomorphic to the usual undirected H1(G,L)H_1(G,L)H1​(G,L).

So right now I feel we don't need an extra arrow to indicate directedness: the fact that the coefficients are a commutative monoid requires directedness, but when the coefficients are an abelian group the homology becomes independent of which way the edges point.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Apr 18 2025 at 20:35):

Thanks. I understand your point.

John Baez (Apr 19 2025 at 01:07):

I'm trying to understand Mayer-Vietoris for graphs, and in particular the all-important boundary map

∂:H1(X∪Y)→H0(X∩Y)\partial: H_1(X \cup Y) \to H_0(X \cap Y)∂:H1​(X∪Y)→H0​(X∩Y)

in a really concrete way when XXX and YYY are subgraphs of a graph X∪YX \cup YX∪Y, and X∩YX \cap YX∩Y is just a set of vertices. I realize I don't understand this map as vividly as I'd like.

I first learned homology theory in a course using William Massey's book Singular Homology Theory, and section III.3 is called "Homology of finite graphs". But it doesn't help me that much since it's mostly about computing the homology of a graph.
